  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
payload.user_prompt:
Write one complete Referral Letter for clinical domain General Medicine.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Gujarati (code=gu, script=Gujarati). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match General Medicine — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Gujarat, district=Sabar Kantha.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Gujarati for Gujarati.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Female
- age: 58
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: Farm Overseer, Crop Production
- education_level: Matric/Secondary
- zone: Rural
- state: Gujarat
- district: Sabar Kantha
- first_language: Gujarati
- clinical_persona_summary: Dahiben Muchadiya is a 58-year-old female farm overseer in Sabar Kantha, Gujarat, married, with a matric education. She works in crop production and is fluent in Gujarati, using Hindi for official tasks. Her lifestyle includes daily walks, seasonal cricket, and a diet rich in traditional Gujarati foods.

Document format guide for Referral Letter (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
Referral [[REFERRAL_ID|REF-2024-01]] from [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]] / [[DOCTOR_NAME|...]]
Re: [[PATIENT_NAME|...]], [[AGE|36]] / [[GENDER|Male]], District [[DISTRICT|...]]
Reason: <domain-appropriate clinical reason matching persona>

MANDATORY inline entities (12 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- REFERRAL_ID — Referral ID e.g. [[REFERRAL_ID|REF-2024-1123]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- RELATIVE_NAME — Relative Name (Father/Mother/Spouse/Guardian) e.g. [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
- PASSPORT_NUMBER — Passport Number e.g. [[PASSPORT_NUMBER|Z1234567]]
- DRIVING_LICENCE — Driving Licence Number e.g. [[DRIVING_LICENCE|UP14 20110012345]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.
payload.generated_text:
Referral [[REFERRAL_ID|REF-2024-01]] from [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Sabar Kantha District Hospital]] / [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Rajesh Patel]]
Re: [[PATIENT_NAME|Dahiben Muchadiya]], [[AGE|58]] / [[GENDER|Female]], District [[DISTRICT|Sabar Kantha]]
Reason: Evaluation and management of uncontrolled hypertension and type 2 diabetes mellitus

Dear Colleague,

I am referring [[PATIENT_NAME|Dahiben Muchadiya]], a 58-year-old female farm overseer from [[VILLAGE|Ranipura]] village in [[DISTRICT|Sabar Kantha]] district, for your expert consultation regarding her chronic medical conditions.

[[PATIENT_NAME|Dahiben Muchadiya]] has been diagnosed with hypertension and type 2 diabetes mellitus for approximately 8 years. Despite being on oral antihypertensive medications and oral hypoglycemic agents, her blood pressure remains consistently elevated (150-160/90-100 mmHg) and fasting blood glucose levels are often above 200 mg/dL. She reports occasional headaches, mild fatigue, and occasional episodes of blurred vision.

[[PATIENT_NAME|Dahiben Muchadiya]] is married and lives with her husband [[RELATIVE_NAME|Rameshbhai Muchadiya]] in their agricultural land. She has a [[BPL_RATION_CARD|BPL-GJ-2024-001234]] and is covered under the Pradhan Mantri Jan Arogya Yojana with [[INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER|PMJAY-GJ-2024-567890]]. Her [[AADHAAR_NUMBER|234567890123]] is linked to her health records.

[[PATIENT_NAME|Dahiben Muchadiya]]'s [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]] is attached for your reference. Her [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]] is available for communication. She can be reached at her residence in [[RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS|Plot No. 15, Agricultural Colony, Ranipura, Sabar Kantha]].

Please find her [[PASSPORT_NUMBER|G1234567]] and [[DRIVING_LICENCE|GJ142020123456]] details for identification purposes. Her [[ABHA_ID|ABHA-1234-5678-9012]] is also registered in the national health stack.

We request your expert opinion on optimizing her antihypertensive and antidiabetic regimen, evaluation for possible complications, and guidance on lifestyle modifications appropriate for her rural agricultural lifestyle.

Thank you for your attention to this case.

Sincerely,
[[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Rajesh Patel]]
General Medicine Specialist
[[HOSPITAL_NAME|Sabar Kantha District Hospital]]
[[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]]
[[EMAIL_ADDRESS|rajesh.patel@skdh.in]]

payload.professional_persona: An experienced rubber tapper and tea garden labourer, Sofikul Sekh leverages their expert bark‑incision technique, manual dexterity and small‑team coordination to maintain steady production, and they prefer the routine and practical approach that their reserved personality provides.
payload.linguistic_persona: A fluent Hindi speaker with functional Bengali conversation skills, Sofikul Sekh relies on oral traditions and visual cues to communicate, as they are illiterate in both languages, preferring routine verbal exchanges over written text.
payload.sports_persona: A casual cricket enthusiast who joins the Jalpaiguri Tea Garden Cricket Club for weekend matches and follows the Kolkata Knight Riders in the IPL, Sofikul Sekh also enjoys early morning walks through the garden lanes, and they prefer routine low‑impact fitness over high‑intensity sports.
payload.arts_persona: A lover of soulful music, Sofikul Sekh frequently tunes into Nusrat Fateh Ali Khan’s qawwalis, Jagjit Singh’s ghazals, and traditional Bengali folk songs during tea‑garden evenings, while they also cherish oral tales of local legends narrated by elders.
payload.travel_persona: A modest traveler who cherishes weekend getaways to nearby hill stations like Darjeeling and Kalimpong, and occasional trips to Kolkata by train, Sofikul Sekh prefers well‑planned, low‑cost itineraries that fit around work commitments and family responsibilities, and they enjoy the quiet of the journey as much as the destination.
payload.culinary_persona: A practical food lover who favors roti with aloo sabzi, dal tadka, and mustard‑oil cooked vegetables from his own garden, Sofikul Sekh enjoys a steaming cup of tea with samosa in the evenings and treats themselves to occasional mutton biryani or paturi fish during festive gatherings, and they value simplicity in meals.
payload.persona: Sofikul Sekh is a routine‑driven, gentle soul who blends skilled manual labour with quiet devotion to faith, family and modest ambition, navigating life with steady practicality; they cherish simplicity and reliability above all.
payload.cultural_background: Born and raised in the rural tea‑garden belt of Jalpaiguri in North Bengal, Sofikul belongs to the Hindi‑speaking Muslim community that settled in the region as part of migration waves during the mid‑20th century. His upbringing is steeped in the agrarian rhythms of the countryside, where seasonal festivals, communal prayers and the simple customs of village life shape daily routines. He observes the local Bengali cultural colour—such as the Durga Puja atmosphere in the surrounding villages—while retaining Hindi culinary habits, dress (kurta‑pyjama) and the Islamic rites that anchor his identity.
payload.linguistic_background: His mother tongue is Hindi, which he speaks fluently and uses for all household communication. He has functional conversational proficiency in Bengali, acquired through daily interaction with neighbours and at the local market, though he cannot read or write in either language due to his illiteracy. His limited exposure to formal education means he relies on oral tradition and visual cues for learning, and he can recognise basic symbols such as numerals, religious signs, and simple instructions.
payload.skills_and_expertise: Expert in rubber tapping, including identifying the right bark incision points and extracting latex efficiently. Proficient in tea garden labour such as plucking tea leaves, pruning, and maintaining the health of tea bushes. Strong physical stamina and manual dexterity developed through years of intense field work. Capable of coordinating small groups of workers for daily tasks, ensuring timely completion while maintaining harmonious workplace relations.
payload.skills_and_expertise_list: ['Rubber tapping', 'Tea leaf plucking', 'Manual labour', 'Team coordination', 'Basic religious duties']
payload.hobbies_and_interests: He enjoys listening to devotional bhajans and qawwalis, tending to his small vegetable garden, participating in communal tea garden cultural evenings, listening to oral tales, and occasionally playing chess with his neighbours.
payload.hobbies_and_interests_list: ['Listening to devotional music', 'Vegetable gardening', 'Participating in community gatherings', 'Oral storytelling', 'Playing chess']
payload.career_goals_and_ambitions: He aspires to secure a steady and slightly higher income by gradually taking on supervisory responsibilities in the tea garden or rubber tapping operations, while also ensuring that his children receive formal education, which he sees as the key to breaking the cycle of illiteracy. He hopes to eventually own a small plot of land where he can cultivate vegetables for his family's consumption, thereby enhancing food security and fostering a modest degree of financial independence.
payload.persona_clinical_summary: A 40-year-old male from Jalpaiguri, West Bengal, who is married and works as a tapper in a rural setting. He is illiterate, speaks Hindi as his first language, and has functional Bengali proficiency. His lifestyle includes manual labor, a preference for routine, and limited formal education.

payload.user_prompt:
Write one complete Automated SMS Notifications for clinical domain Tuberculosis & Non-Communicable Diseases.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Hindi (code=hi, script=Devanagari). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Tuberculosis & Non-Communicable Diseases — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=West Bengal, district=Jalpaiguri.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Devanagari for Hindi.

LENGTH HARD LIMIT: entire SMS ≤ ~500 characters, but you MUST still include every mandatory entity tag below at least once (compact tags OK).

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Male
- age: 40
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: Tappers, Other
- education_level: Illiterate
- zone: Rural
- state: West Bengal
- district: Jalpaiguri
- first_language: Hindi
- clinical_persona_summary: A 40-year-old male from Jalpaiguri, West Bengal, who is married and works as a tapper in a rural setting. He is illiterate, speaks Hindi as his first language, and has functional Bengali proficiency. His lifestyle includes manual labor, a preference for routine, and limited formal education.

Document format guide for Automated SMS Notifications (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
CRITICAL: keep the WHOLE SMS under ~400 characters / 2–4 sentences. Not a form dump.
MUST tag [[APPOINTMENT_ID|…]] whenever an appointment is mentioned.
Example:
[[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]: appt [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]] at [[HOSPITAL_NAME|PHC Kanpur]] on 21-May 10:30 with [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Sharma]]. Confirm on [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]. MRN [[MRN|MRN-2024-001]].

MANDATORY inline entities (6 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- APPOINTMENT_ID — Appointment ID e.g. [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

payload.professional_persona: An experienced rubber tapper and tea garden labourer, Sofikul Sekh leverages their expert bark‑incision technique, manual dexterity and small‑team coordination to maintain steady production, and they prefer the routine and practical approach that their reserved personality provides.
payload.linguistic_persona: A fluent Hindi speaker with functional Bengali conversation skills, Sofikul Sekh relies on oral traditions and visual cues to communicate, as they are illiterate in both languages, preferring routine verbal exchanges over written text.
payload.sports_persona: A casual cricket enthusiast who joins the Jalpaiguri Tea Garden Cricket Club for weekend matches and follows the Kolkata Knight Riders in the IPL, Sofikul Sekh also enjoys early morning walks through the garden lanes, and they prefer routine low‑impact fitness over high‑intensity sports.
payload.arts_persona: A lover of soulful music, Sofikul Sekh frequently tunes into Nusrat Fateh Ali Khan’s qawwalis, Jagjit Singh’s ghazals, and traditional Bengali folk songs during tea‑garden evenings, while they also cherish oral tales of local legends narrated by elders.
payload.travel_persona: A modest traveler who cherishes weekend getaways to nearby hill stations like Darjeeling and Kalimpong, and occasional trips to Kolkata by train, Sofikul Sekh prefers well‑planned, low‑cost itineraries that fit around work commitments and family responsibilities, and they enjoy the quiet of the journey as much as the destination.
payload.culinary_persona: A practical food lover who favors roti with aloo sabzi, dal tadka, and mustard‑oil cooked vegetables from his own garden, Sofikul Sekh enjoys a steaming cup of tea with samosa in the evenings and treats themselves to occasional mutton biryani or paturi fish during festive gatherings, and they value simplicity in meals.
payload.persona: Sofikul Sekh is a routine‑driven, gentle soul who blends skilled manual labour with quiet devotion to faith, family and modest ambition, navigating life with steady practicality; they cherish simplicity and reliability above all.
payload.cultural_background: Born and raised in the rural tea‑garden belt of Jalpaiguri in North Bengal, Sofikul belongs to the Hindi‑speaking Muslim community that settled in the region as part of migration waves during the mid‑20th century. His upbringing is steeped in the agrarian rhythms of the countryside, where seasonal festivals, communal prayers and the simple customs of village life shape daily routines. He observes the local Bengali cultural colour—such as the Durga Puja atmosphere in the surrounding villages—while retaining Hindi culinary habits, dress (kurta‑pyjama) and the Islamic rites that anchor his identity.
payload.linguistic_background: His mother tongue is Hindi, which he speaks fluently and uses for all household communication. He has functional conversational proficiency in Bengali, acquired through daily interaction with neighbours and at the local market, though he cannot read or write in either language due to his illiteracy. His limited exposure to formal education means he relies on oral tradition and visual cues for learning, and he can recognise basic symbols such as numerals, religious signs, and simple instructions.
payload.skills_and_expertise: Expert in rubber tapping, including identifying the right bark incision points and extracting latex efficiently. Proficient in tea garden labour such as plucking tea leaves, pruning, and maintaining the health of tea bushes. Strong physical stamina and manual dexterity developed through years of intense field work. Capable of coordinating small groups of workers for daily tasks, ensuring timely completion while maintaining harmonious workplace relations.
payload.skills_and_expertise_list: ['Rubber tapping', 'Tea leaf plucking', 'Manual labour', 'Team coordination', 'Basic religious duties']
payload.hobbies_and_interests: He enjoys listening to devotional bhajans and qawwalis, tending to his small vegetable garden, participating in communal tea garden cultural evenings, listening to oral tales, and occasionally playing chess with his neighbours.
payload.hobbies_and_interests_list: ['Listening to devotional music', 'Vegetable gardening', 'Participating in community gatherings', 'Oral storytelling', 'Playing chess']
payload.career_goals_and_ambitions: He aspires to secure a steady and slightly higher income by gradually taking on supervisory responsibilities in the tea garden or rubber tapping operations, while also ensuring that his children receive formal education, which he sees as the key to breaking the cycle of illiteracy. He hopes to eventually own a small plot of land where he can cultivate vegetables for his family's consumption, thereby enhancing food security and fostering a modest degree of financial independence.
payload.persona_clinical_summary: Mr. Sofikul Sekh, a 40-year-old male, is a rural tapper in West Bengal’s Jalpaiguri district, married with no formal education. He speaks Hindi at home and Bengali for daily interactions, and works in manual labor. His lifestyle includes a modest diet of roti, dal, and garden vegetables, with occasional tea and samosa. He prefers routine and practical approaches to daily life.

You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Use ONLY allow-list TYPE names. Billing aliases: EMAIL/CONTACT_EMAIL → EMAIL_ADDRESS; ADDRESS_NUMBER → RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS; RECEIPT_NUMBER and STATE are NOT types (receipt no. / state stay plain prose; geography uses DISTRICT).

- PAN must match ^[A-Z]{5}[0-9]{4}[A-Z]$ e.g. ABCDE1234F — not KAAPR1234C-style junk.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Maternal_health must be age-plausible: routine ANC/delivery for ages ~18–40; ages 40–42 → high-risk ANC / PNC / contraception — never invent extreme late pregnancy (e.g. 49yo as routine 28-week gestation).

- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content;
  geriatric / extreme-age routine 28-week pregnancy.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; maternal content is
  age-plausible (no unsafe extreme late pregnancy as a routine story).

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs (billing: EMAIL→EMAIL_ADDRESS, ADDRESS→RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS;
  receipt/state/money stay plain prose). Valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.

payload.system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Use ONLY allow-list TYPE names. Billing aliases: EMAIL/CONTACT_EMAIL → EMAIL_ADDRESS; ADDRESS_NUMBER → RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS; RECEIPT_NUMBER and STATE are NOT types (receipt no. / state stay plain prose; geography uses DISTRICT).

- PAN must match ^[A-Z]{5}[0-9]{4}[A-Z]$ e.g. ABCDE1234F — not KAAPR1234C-style junk.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Maternal_health must be age-plausible: routine ANC/delivery for ages ~18–40; ages 40–42 → high-risk ANC / PNC / contraception — never invent extreme late pregnancy (e.g. 49yo as routine 28-week gestation).

- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Format examples (copy the bracket pattern; invent new synthetic values):
  [[AADHAAR_NUMBER|157815659387]]
  [[ABHA_ADDRESS|meena.devi@abdm]]
  [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]

  [[BPL_RATION_CARD|BPL-UP-208001-4421]]

  [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]

  [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-77821]]

  [[HOSPITAL_ID|HSP-UP-014]]

  [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]
  [[INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER|POL-PB-2024-7781]]

Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content;
  geriatric / extreme-age routine 28-week pregnancy.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; maternal content is
  age-plausible (no unsafe extreme late pregnancy as a routine story).

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs (billing: EMAIL→EMAIL_ADDRESS, ADDRESS→RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS;
  receipt/state/money stay plain prose). Valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
payload.user_prompt:
Write one complete Insurance Claim / TPA Form for clinical domain Tuberculosis & Non-Communicable Diseases.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Hindi (code=hi, script=Devanagari). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Tuberculosis & Non-Communicable Diseases — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Madhya Pradesh, district=Mandsaur.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Devanagari for Hindi.

PLACEMENT (vehicle): Put VEHICLE_REGISTRATION exactly once (parking/ambulance or motor claim line). Do not dump or repeat it.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Male
- age: 42
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: Teacher, Infant School Pre-Primary Education Teaching Associate Professionals, Other
- education_level: Graduate & above
- zone: Urban
- state: Madhya Pradesh
- district: Mandsaur
- first_language: Hindi
- clinical_persona_summary: Mr. Shiv Lal, a 42-year-old male teacher in pre-primary education, is currently married and resides in urban Mandsaur, Madhya Pradesh. He holds a graduate degree and speaks Hindi as his first language, with functional English proficiency. His lifestyle includes daily yoga, puja, and tending to a mango tree, reflecting a disciplined yet flexible routine.

Document format guide for Insurance Claim / TPA Form (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
TPA claim — Policy [[INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER|POL-PB-2024-7781]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|...]] [[AGE|25]] [[GENDER|Male]] Aadhaar [[AADHAAR_NUMBER|157815659387]]
Hospital [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]] District [[DISTRICT|...]]
Motor / RTA vehicle [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|PB10AB1234]] (exactly once).
PAN [[PAN_NUMBER|ABCDE1234F]] IFSC [[IFSC_CODE|SBIN0001234]] account [[BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER|...]]

MANDATORY inline entities (16 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER — Insurance Policy Number e.g. [[INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER|POL-PB-2024-7781]]
- AADHAAR_NUMBER — Aadhaar Number e.g. [[AADHAAR_NUMBER|157815659387]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- PAN_NUMBER — PAN Number e.g. [[PAN_NUMBER|ABCDE1234F]]
- BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER — Bank Account Number e.g. [[BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER|50200012345678]]
- IFSC_CODE — IFSC Code e.g. [[IFSC_CODE|SBIN0001234]]
- BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER — Bank Routing Number e.g. [[BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER|SBIN0001234]]
- CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER — Credit Card Number e.g. [[CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER|4111111111111111]]
- CVV — CVV e.g. [[CVV|123]]
- PIN — PIN e.g. [[PIN|4829]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION — Vehicle Registration Number e.g. [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|UP78AB1234]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.
payload.generated_text:
TPA Claim Form — Policy [[INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER|POL-MP-2024-9876]]
Patient Details:
[[PATIENT_NAME|Shiv Lal]] [[AGE|42]] [[GENDER|Male]] [[MRN|MRN-2024-0515-004]] Aadhaar [[AADHAAR_NUMBER|203835321155]]
[[PHONE_NUMBER|9425098765]]
[[PIN|458001]] [[DISTRICT|Mandsaur]]
[[PAN_NUMBER|ABCDE1234F]]

Hospital Details:
Admitted at [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Shri Mahadev Hospital]] [[DISTRICT|Mandsaur]] on 15th May 2024.
[[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-0515-004]]
[[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Rajesh Patel]]

Clinical Summary:
Patient Mr. Shiv Lal, a 42-year-old male teacher, presented with a 3-week history of persistent dry cough, low-grade fever, and significant weight loss. He has a known history of Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus, controlled with Metformin. Investigations revealed a positive Mantoux test and chest X-ray showing infiltrates in the right upper lobe, consistent with Pulmonary Tuberculosis. Sputum for AFB was positive. The patient was started on Category I Anti-Tubercular Therapy (ATT) and his diabetes management was optimized. He was discharged on 25th May 2024 with a prescription for a 6-month course of ATT.

Bank Details for Reimbursement:
Account Holder Name: Shiv Lal
Bank Name: State Bank of India
[[BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER|50200012345678]]
[[IFSC_CODE|SBIN0001234]]
[[BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER|SBIN0001234]]
[[CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER|4111111111111111]] [[CVV|123]]

Motor / RTA Vehicle Details:
The patient was brought to the hospital via ambulance. [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|MP04AB1234]]

Contact Information:
For any queries, please contact the TPA at 1800-123-4567 or email at support@tpainsurance.com.

payload.linguistic_persona: Shiv Lal, a native Hindi speaker fully literate in Hindi, possesses functional English skills that suffice for reading textbooks, preparing lesson plans and occasional emails, preferring Hindi for most interactions while they use English only when needed.
payload.sports_persona: Shiv Lal, a dedicated bowler in local cricket matches who supports the Madhya Pradesh cricket team and plays for the Mandsaur Cricket Club, enjoys Saturday morning practice at the community ground, balances a competitive spirit with a relaxed fitness routine, and they complement cricket with regular yoga sessions for flexibility.
payload.arts_persona: Shiv Lal, an avid reader of Hindi literature who delights in the poetry of Gopal Das Nepali and the novels of Phanishwar Nath Renu, frequently quotes verses during conversations, enjoys classic Bollywood melodies by Kishore Kumar, and they attend occasional folk music gatherings featuring Malwa’s regional singers.
payload.travel_persona: Shiv Lal, who enjoys weekend getaways to nearby heritage gems like the lakes of Udaipur, the temples of Khajuraho, and the historic forts of Gwalior, plans trips with a mix of detailed itineraries and spontaneous detours, and they dream of a peaceful retreat in the Himalayan hill stations such as Shimla, balancing family responsibilities and modest budget.
payload.culinary_persona: Shiv Lal, who relishes the tangy zest of aam ka achar, the buttery flavor of ghee‑roasted bhindi, the comforting warmth of dal‑tadka with fresh coriander, enjoys a steady cup of masala chai in the evenings, can whip up simple roti‑dal meals but often orders paneer tikka from the local dhaba, and they have a soft spot for sweets like jalebi and besan laddu.
payload.persona: Shiv Lal, a calm yet competitively driven pre‑primary teacher who blends structured lesson planning with spontaneous creativity, balances daily puja, yoga, and mango‑tree tending, and they manage a disciplined yet flexible approach to life.
payload.cultural_background: Shiv Lal hails from Mandsaur, a historic town in the Malwa region of Madhya Pradesh. He comes from a middle‑class, Hindi‑speaking family that has deep roots in the agricultural and small‑business community of the city. The local culture blends traditional festivals like Navratri with a pragmatic outlook shaped by the region's agrarian cycles.
payload.linguistic_background: Hindi is Shiv’s mother‑tongue and he is fully literate in it, using it for both personal and professional communication. He possesses functional proficiency in English – sufficient for reading textbooks, preparing lesson plans, and occasional correspondence with parents and school administration – but does not use English regularly in daily conversation.
payload.skills_and_expertise: Shiv possesses strong early‑childhood pedagogical skills, including child‑centered lesson planning, classroom management, and basic child psychology. He is adept at communicating with parents in Hindi, preparing instructional materials, and using simple digital tools for interactive learning. His experience also includes organizing school events and coordinating with local educational authorities.
payload.skills_and_expertise_list: ['Early childhood lesson planning', 'Classroom management', 'Basic child psychology', 'Parent communication (Hindi)', 'Instructional material design', 'Digital teaching tools', 'Event organization', 'Coordination with educational authorities']
payload.hobbies_and_interests: In his leisure time, Shiv enjoys reading Hindi literature, especially poetry by Gopal Das Nepali and novels by Phanishwar Nath Renu. He practices yoga and simple meditation to maintain his calm demeanor. He also likes gardening – tending to mango and neem trees in his backyard – and occasionally participates in local cricket matches as a bowler.
payload.hobbies_and_interests_list: ['Reading Hindi literature', 'Yoga and meditation', 'Gardening (mango & neem trees)', 'Playing cricket (bowler)']
payload.career_goals_and_ambitions: Shiv aims to advance from classroom teaching to a supervisory role, such as Head of Pre‑Primary Section, where he can mentor younger teachers and shape curriculum standards. He aspires to pursue a postgraduate diploma in Early Childhood Education, integrate more technology‑enabled pedagogy, and eventually open a community‑run child care centre that serves under‑privileged families in Mandsaur.
payload.occupation: Teacher, Infant School Pre-Primary Education Teaching Associate Professionals, Other
payload.persona_clinical_summary: Mr. Shiv Lal, a 42-year-old male teacher in infant school pre-primary education, is married and resides in urban Madhya Pradesh, Mandsaur district. He is a graduate with a Hindi-speaking background and works in a structured yet flexible teaching role. His lifestyle includes daily puja, yoga, and tending to a mango tree, reflecting a disciplined and culturally rooted routine.

payload.user_prompt:
Write one complete Automated SMS Notifications for clinical domain Surgical.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Hindi (code=hi, script=Devanagari). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Surgical — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Madhya Pradesh, district=Mandsaur.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Devanagari for Hindi.

LENGTH HARD LIMIT: entire SMS ≤ ~500 characters, but you MUST still include every mandatory entity tag below at least once (compact tags OK).

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Male
- age: 42
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: Teacher, Infant School Pre-Primary Education Teaching Associate Professionals, Other
- education_level: Graduate & above
- zone: Urban
- state: Madhya Pradesh
- district: Mandsaur
- first_language: Hindi
- clinical_persona_summary: Mr. Shiv Lal, a 42-year-old male teacher in infant school pre-primary education, is married and resides in urban Madhya Pradesh, Mandsaur district. He is a graduate with a Hindi-speaking background and works in a structured yet flexible teaching role. His lifestyle includes daily puja, yoga, and tending to a mango tree, reflecting a disciplined and culturally rooted routine.

Document format guide for Automated SMS Notifications (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
CRITICAL: keep the WHOLE SMS under ~400 characters / 2–4 sentences. Not a form dump.
MUST tag [[APPOINTMENT_ID|…]] whenever an appointment is mentioned.
Example:
[[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]: appt [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]] at [[HOSPITAL_NAME|PHC Kanpur]] on 21-May 10:30 with [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Sharma]]. Confirm on [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]. MRN [[MRN|MRN-2024-001]].

MANDATORY inline entities (6 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- APPOINTMENT_ID — Appointment ID e.g. [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

payload.arts_persona: Shiv Lal, an avid reader of Hindi literature who delights in the poetry of Gopal Das Nepali and the novels of Phanishwar Nath Renu, frequently quotes verses during conversations, enjoys classic Bollywood melodies by Kishore Kumar, and they attend occasional folk music gatherings featuring Malwa’s regional singers.

payload.culinary_persona: Shiv Lal, who relishes the tangy zest of aam ka achar, the buttery flavor of ghee‑roasted bhindi, the comforting warmth of dal‑tadka with fresh coriander, enjoys a steady cup of masala chai in the evenings, can whip up simple roti‑dal meals but often orders paneer tikka from the local dhaba, and they have a soft spot for sweets like jalebi and besan laddu.
payload.persona: Shiv Lal, a calm yet competitively driven pre‑primary teacher who blends structured lesson planning with spontaneous creativity, balances daily puja, yoga, and mango‑tree tending, and they manage a disciplined yet flexible approach to life.
payload.cultural_background: Shiv Lal hails from Mandsaur, a historic town in the Malwa region of Madhya Pradesh. He comes from a middle‑class, Hindi‑speaking family that has deep roots in the agricultural and small‑business community of the city. The local culture blends traditional festivals like Navratri with a pragmatic outlook shaped by the region's agrarian cycles.
payload.linguistic_background: Hindi is Shiv’s mother‑tongue and he is fully literate in it, using it for both personal and professional communication. He possesses functional proficiency in English – sufficient for reading textbooks, preparing lesson plans, and occasional correspondence with parents and school administration – but does not use English regularly in daily conversation.
payload.skills_and_expertise: Shiv possesses strong early‑childhood pedagogical skills, including child‑centered lesson planning, classroom management, and basic child psychology. He is adept at communicating with parents in Hindi, preparing instructional materials, and using simple digital tools for interactive learning. His experience also includes organizing school events and coordinating with local educational authorities.
payload.skills_and_expertise_list: ['Early childhood lesson planning', 'Classroom management', 'Basic child psychology', 'Parent communication (Hindi)', 'Instructional material design', 'Digital teaching tools', 'Event organization', 'Coordination with educational authorities']
payload.hobbies_and_interests: In his leisure time, Shiv enjoys reading Hindi literature, especially poetry by Gopal Das Nepali and novels by Phanishwar Nath Renu. He practices yoga and simple meditation to maintain his calm demeanor. He also likes gardening – tending to mango and neem trees in his backyard – and occasionally participates in local cricket matches as a bowler.
payload.hobbies_and_interests_list: ['Reading Hindi literature', 'Yoga and meditation', 'Gardening (mango & neem trees)', 'Playing cricket (bowler)']
payload.career_goals_and_ambitions: Shiv aims to advance from classroom teaching to a supervisory role, such as Head of Pre‑Primary Section, where he can mentor younger teachers and shape curriculum standards. He aspires to pursue a postgraduate diploma in Early Childhood Education, integrate more technology‑enabled pedagogy, and eventually open a community‑run child care centre that serves under‑privileged families in Mandsaur.
payload.occupation: Teacher, Infant School Pre-Primary Education Teaching Associate Professionals, Other
payload.persona_clinical_summary: Mr. Shiv Lal, a 42-year-old male teacher in pre-primary education, is currently married, holds a graduate degree, and resides in urban Mandsaur, Madhya Pradesh. He is Hindi-speaking with functional English proficiency, and his lifestyle includes daily yoga, puja, and tending to a mango tree. payload.professional_persona: Pandurang Godekar is a seasoned farmer who blends deep knowledge of millet, pulses and animal husbandry with market negotiation skills, and they are actively pursuing solar‑drip irrigation and a small diesel tractor to modernise their farm while championing a farmer’s cooperative in Gadag.
payload.linguistic_persona: Pandurang Godekar speaks fluent Kannada, picks up functional Hindi and basic English through years of market chatter, yet they remain illiterate, relying on oral tradition, folk sayings, and radio programmes to navigate daily transactions and agricultural advice.
payload.sports_persona: Pandurang Godekar follows Karnataka’s cricket team with fervor, attends local Kabaddi matches during village fairs, and they maintain personal fitness through early‑morning cattle walks and occasional sessions of traditional wrestling (kusti) with fellow farmers.
payload.arts_persona: Pandurang Godekar is a devoted fan of Karnataka folk singer K. V. Gopal and bhajan maestro Shivraj Bhat, they regularly tune into Yakshagana recordings featuring legend Dr. Karanth, and often hum these melodies while tending their fields.
payload.travel_persona: Pandurang Godekar prefers short, purpose‑driven trips to nearby towns like Hubli and Bengaluru for market dealings and their sons’ school visits, yet they dream of a pilgrimage to Varanasi and a leisurely stay at Mysore Palace during the Dasara festival when finances permit.
payload.culinary_persona: Pandurang Godekar favors hearty jowar rotti served with spicy sambar made from toor dal, drumsticks, and tamarind, they occasionally prepare coconut‑based chutney with coriander, and delight in the occasional lemon‑rice (pulao) with peanuts during village fairs.
payload.persona: Pandurang Godekar, a 49‑year‑old farmer who blends curiosity with practical ‘jugaad’, they worry about market swings yet tirelessly work to modernise their fields while keeping village traditions alive.
payload.cultural_background: Pandurang comes from a traditional agrarian community in Gadag, a historic town in northern Karnataka. His family follows Kannada customs, celebrating Ugadi, Sankranti, and local temple festivals with folk dances like Yakshagana and devotional music. Being a Hindu in a rural setting, he participates in community rituals at the village shrine and observes seasonal harvest ceremonies. The regional culture also values practical ingenuity - the age-old 'jugaad' mindset that influences his farming practices.
payload.linguistic_background: Pandurang's mother tongue is Kannada, which he speaks fluently and uses for daily communication with his family and neighbours. He cannot read or write in any language, but his decades of market interaction have given him functional spoken English and Hindi, enabling him to negotiate prices, understand basic instructions, and follow radio programmes about agriculture. He relies on oral tradition and memory, often using folk sayings to convey complex ideas. His linguistic repertoire reflects a pragmatic blend of regional, national, and market languages.
payload.skills_and_expertise: Pandurang possesses deep practical knowledge of rain-fed and irrigated crop production, especially millet, pulses, and oilseeds native to Karnataka. He is adept at animal husbandry, managing cattle and goats for milk and draught power. Over the years he has honed market-oriented skills such as price negotiation, product grading, and leveraging local traders to sell produce. He can maintain basic farm machinery, repair bullock-drawn ploughs, and apply low-cost pest control using neem and trap crops. His expertise also includes seasonal planning based on monsoon forecasts and maintaining seed banks of traditional varieties.
payload.skills_and_expertise_list: ['Crop cultivation (millet, pulses, oilseeds)', 'Animal husbandry (cattle, goats)', 'Market negotiation and pricing', 'Product grading and quality control', 'Basic farm machinery maintenance', 'Low-cost pest control using neem and trap crops', 'Seasonal planning based on monsoon forecasts', 'Seed preservation of traditional varieties', 'Community coordination for festivals and fairs', 'Oral communication and memory retention']
payload.hobbies_and_interests: Pandurang enjoys listening to Karnataka folk songs and bhajans on the radio, especially those dedicated to Lord Shiva. He spends his free time preparing traditional dishes like jowar rotti and sambar for family gatherings. He walks his cattle in the fields at dawn, which he finds therapeutic. He is fond of attending village fairs during Sankranti and Diwali, where he watches folk performances and participates in games. In quieter moments, he likes reciting simple mantras and reminiscing about his youth through storytelling.
payload.hobbies_and_interests_list: ['Listening to Karnataka folk songs and bhajans', 'Preparing traditional dishes (jowar roti, sambar)', 'Morning walks with cattle', 'Attending village fairs during Sankranti and Diwali', 'Reciting simple mantras and storytelling', 'Watching folk performances', 'Playing community games', 'Talking with fellow farmers about market trends']
payload.career_goals_and_ambitions: Pandurang aims to modernise his farm while preserving his ancestral knowledge. He dreams of acquiring a small diesel tractor and solar-powered drip irrigation to increase yields and reduce water usage. He wants to establish a farmer’s cooperative in Gadag to collectively market produce, negotiate better prices, and access credit facilities. Ensuring that his two sons receive formal education is a personal priority, so he hopes to provide them with enough income to send them to school. Ultimately, he aspires to become a respected elder in his village, guiding younger farmers on sustainable practices and safeguarding the local grain varieties.
payload.occupation: Market-Oriented Crop and Animal Producers, Other
payload.persona_clinical_summary: A 49-year-old male farmer from Gadag, Karnataka, currently married, with an illiterate education level, works in market-oriented crop and animal production. His first language is Kannada, and he resides in a rural zone. He is concerned about market fluctuations and is actively seeking to modernize his farming practices.
payload.system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Use ONLY allow-list TYPE names. Billing aliases: EMAIL/CONTACT_EMAIL → EMAIL_ADDRESS; ADDRESS_NUMBER → RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS; RECEIPT_NUMBER and STATE are NOT types (receipt no. / state stay plain prose; geography uses DISTRICT).

- PAN must match ^[A-Z]{5}[0-9]{4}[A-Z]$ e.g. ABCDE1234F — not KAAPR1234C-style junk.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Maternal_health must be age-plausible: routine ANC/delivery for ages ~18–40; ages 40–42 → high-risk ANC / PNC / contraception — never invent extreme late pregnancy (e.g. 49yo as routine 28-week gestation).

- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Format examples (copy the bracket pattern; invent new synthetic values):

  [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]

  [[GENDER|Female]]
  [[HOSPITAL_ID|HSP-UP-014]]
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
  [[IFSC_CODE|SBIN0001234]]
  [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]
  [[INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER|POL-PB-2024-7781]]

  [[MAC_ADDRESS|00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E]]

  [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|UP78AB1234]]

Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content;
  geriatric / extreme-age routine 28-week pregnancy.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; maternal content is
  age-plausible (no unsafe extreme late pregnancy as a routine story).

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs (billing: EMAIL→EMAIL_ADDRESS, ADDRESS→RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS;
  receipt/state/money stay plain prose). Valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.

Write one complete OPD Slip (Outpatient Department) for clinical domain General Medicine.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Kannada (code=kn, script=Kannada). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.

ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match General Medicine — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Karnataka, district=Gadag.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Kannada for Kannada.

PLACEMENT (OPD header): First line MUST include BOTH [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] (facility name) AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] (facility code) — never substitute one for the other.
Put [[EMPLOYEE_ID|…]] on the registrar/clerk line and [[RELATIVE_NAME|…]] on the attendant/relative line. Include [[DOB|YYYY-MM-DD]] and [[OCCUPATION|…]] with demographics.

OPD Slip | [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Gadag District Hospital]] | ID [[HOSPITAL_ID|GDS-2024-001]]
Patient: [[PATIENT_NAME|Basavaraj]] | DOB [[DOB|1975-07-22]] | Age: [[AGE|49]] | Gender: [[GENDER|Male]]
Occupation: [[OCCUPATION|Farmer]] | MRN: [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]] | Doctor: [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Ramesh Hegde]]
Attendant: [[RELATIVE_NAME|Shakuntala]] | Phone: [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]]
Registrar EmpID: [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-4412]] | District: [[DISTRICT|Gadag]]
Chief complaint: Persistent cough and low-grade fever for the past two weeks.
History of present illness: Patient is a 49-year-old male farmer who presents with a cough and fever. Symptoms started about two weeks ago, initially mild, but have progressively worsened. He reports associated body aches and occasional chills. He attributes the onset to increased exposure to dust while working in his fields.
On examination, the patient is conscious and oriented. Vital signs are stable. General examination reveals mild pallor. Respiratory system examination shows decreased breath sounds in the right lower chest.
A chest X-ray was ordered, which shows a consolidation in the right lower lobe.
Diagnosis: Community-acquired pneumonia, right lower lobe.
Plan: Started on oral Amoxicillin 500mg TID for 5 days. Advised to get adequate rest, maintain hydration, and take paracetamol for fever. Follow-up appointment scheduled in 3 days. Patient counseled on the importance of completing the antibiotic course.
