payload.system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Use ONLY allow-list TYPE names. Billing aliases: EMAIL/CONTACT_EMAIL → EMAIL_ADDRESS; ADDRESS_NUMBER → RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS; RECEIPT_NUMBER and STATE are NOT types (receipt no. / state stay plain prose; geography uses DISTRICT).

- PAN must match ^[A-Z]{5}[0-9]{4}[A-Z]$ e.g. ABCDE1234F — not KAAPR1234C-style junk.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Maternal_health must be age-plausible: routine ANC/delivery for ages ~18–40; ages 40–42 → high-risk ANC / PNC / contraception — never invent extreme late pregnancy (e.g. 49yo as routine 28-week gestation).

- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Format examples (copy the bracket pattern; invent new synthetic values):
  [[AADHAAR_NUMBER|157815659387]]
  [[ABHA_ADDRESS|meena.devi@abdm]]
  [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]
  [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-0912]]
  [[AGE|34]]
  [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]]
  [[ASHA_WORKER_NAME|Sunita Yadav]]
  [[BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER|50200012345678]]
  [[BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER|SBIN0001234]]
  [[BED_NUMBER|12]]
  [[BPL_RATION_CARD|BPL-UP-208001-4421]]
  [[CASTE|OBC]]
  [[CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER|4111111111111111]]
  [[CVV|123]]
  [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
  [[DOB|1992-03-14]]
  [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
  [[DRIVING_LICENCE|UP14 20110012345]]
  [[EMAIL_ADDRESS|meena.devi@example.com]]
  [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-77821]]
  [[ENCOUNTER_ID|ENC-55601]]

  [[HOSPITAL_ID|HSP-UP-014]]

  [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]

  [[URL|https://example.org/u/meena]]
  [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|UP78AB1234]]
  [[VILLAGE|Shivpur]]
  [[VOTER_ID|ABC1234567]]
  [[WARD_NUMBER|B2]]

Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content;
  geriatric / extreme-age routine 28-week pregnancy.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; maternal content is
  age-plausible (no unsafe extreme late pregnancy as a routine story).

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs (billing: EMAIL→EMAIL_ADDRESS, ADDRESS→RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS;
  receipt/state/money stay plain prose). Valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
payload.user_prompt:
Write one complete OPD Slip (Outpatient Department) for clinical domain General Medicine.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Konkani (code=kok, script=Devanagari). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match General Medicine — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Maharashtra, district=Jalgaon.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Devanagari for Konkani.

PLACEMENT (OPD header): First line MUST include BOTH [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] (facility name) AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] (facility code) — never substitute one for the other.
Put [[EMPLOYEE_ID|…]] on the registrar/clerk line and [[RELATIVE_NAME|…]] on the attendant/relative line. Include [[DOB|YYYY-MM-DD]] and [[OCCUPATION|…]] with demographics.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Female
- age: 34
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: Mono Operator
- education_level: Illiterate
- zone: Rural
- state: Maharashtra
- district: Jalgaon
- first_language: Konkani
- clinical_persona_summary: Lalita Tirvadekar is a 34‑year‑old female from a Konkani‑speaking, currently married, rural resident of Jalgaon, Maharashtra, with a mono‑operator occupation and illiterate education. She manages anxiety and community rituals, lives on disciplined savings, and has strong oral communication skills in Konkani and Marathi. Her lifestyle includes early morning walks, casual cricket, and engagement with local cultural activities.

Document format guide for OPD Slip (Outpatient Department) (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton (adapt language/script; keep tags):
OPD Slip | [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital X]] | ID [[HOSPITAL_ID|DH-X-01]]
Patient: [[PATIENT_NAME|...]] | DOB [[DOB|1992-03-14]] | Age: [[AGE|34]] | Gender: [[GENDER|Female]]
Occupation: [[OCCUPATION|Teacher]] | MRN: [[MRN|OPD-2024-001]] | Doctor: [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. ...]]
Relative: [[RELATIVE_NAME|...]] | Phone: [[PHONE_NUMBER|98XXXXXXXX]]
Registrar EmpID: [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-4412]] | District: [[DISTRICT|...]]
Chief complaint: <short clinical line matching assigned domain>

MANDATORY inline entities (13 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- DOB — Date of Birth e.g. [[DOB|1992-03-14]]
- OCCUPATION — Occupation e.g. [[OCCUPATION|Teacher]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- HOSPITAL_ID — Hospital ID e.g. [[HOSPITAL_ID|HSP-UP-014]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- RELATIVE_NAME — Relative Name (Father/Mother/Spouse/Guardian) e.g. [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
- EMPLOYEE_ID — Employee ID e.g. [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-77821]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.
payload.generated_text:
OPD Slip | [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Jalgaon Civil Hospital]] | ID [[HOSPITAL_ID|HSP-MH-JLG-001]]
Patient: [[PATIENT_NAME|Lalita Tirvadekar]] | DOB [[DOB|1990-05-22]] | Age: [[AGE|34]] | Gender: [[GENDER|Female]]
Occupation: [[OCCUPATION|Mono Operator]] | MRN: [[MRN|OPD-JLG-2024-0876]] | Doctor: [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Prakash Patil]]
Relative: [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Tirvadekar]] | Phone: [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]]
Registrar EmpID: [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-JLG-045]] | District: [[DISTRICT|Jalgaon]]
Chief complaint: Persistent cough and low-grade fever for the past five days.
History of present illness: Patient reports a dry cough that has become productive with scanty white sputum over the last two days. She also complains of mild body aches and headache. No shortness of breath noted. She has been taking paracetamol from a local medical shop with partial relief.
On examination, vitals are stable. Temperature is 99.2°F, pulse 88/min, BP 120/80 mmHg. Chest examination reveals decreased breath sounds in the right lower zone with occasional rhonchi. No wheezing or crepts.
Investigations ordered: Complete blood count (CBC), C-reactive protein (CRP), and a chest X-ray.
Provisional diagnosis: Acute lower respiratory tract infection, likely viral.
Treatment plan: Prescribed oral Azithromycin 500mg once daily for 3 days, and oral Paracetamol 500mg as needed for fever and pain. Advised to take rest, increase fluid intake, and follow up in 3 days or sooner if symptoms worsen. Provided a prescription for the ordered investigations.

payload.professional_persona: Lalita Tirvadekar, a mono line operator in a rural telecom network, combines sharp manual dexterity and an ear for line quality with vivid storytelling to explain technical steps; they keep a relaxed, flexible approach that sometimes makes their schedule a bit disorganized.
payload.linguistic_persona: Lalita Tirvadekar, raised speaking Konkani at home and Marathi in school, is illiterate but masters oral communication; they effortlessly switch between both languages and can follow Hindi instructions, using vivid storytelling to bridge any gaps.
payload.sports_persona: Lalita Tirvadekar, who enjoys early morning walks through her vegetable garden and participates in casual cricket games at the village ground, cheerfully follows the Maharashtra women’s cricket team and joins a local women’s volleyball club for weekly fitness.
payload.arts_persona: Lalita Tirvadekar, an enthusiastic creator of abstract folk motifs on fabric and paper, spends evenings immersed in the ragas of Pandit Bhimsen Joshi and the tabla rhythms of Ustad Zakir Hussain, while practicing lively Lavani steps during village celebrations, and draws inspiration from the vivid colors of local Kalamkari artisans.
payload.travel_persona: Lalita Tirvadekar, who prefers spontaneous, budget‑friendly getaways, has explored the misty hills of Mahabaleshwar, the sandy beaches of Goa, and the ancient forts of Raigad; they often travel with a small group of friends during festival holidays to combine cultural immersion with relaxed sightseeing.
payload.culinary_persona: Lalita Tirvadekar, who delights in the sweet aroma of fresh coconut in dishes like sol kadhi, loves the tangy heat of misal pav and the festive sweetness of modak, often prepares simple vangi bhaji at home using brinjal and tamarind, yet prefers to savor street‑food specialties such as vada pav and bhel puri during village fairs.
payload.persona: Lalita Tirvadekar is a 34‑year‑old mono‑line operator who weaves technical precision with vibrant folk art, thrives on community rituals yet juggles anxiety‑driven moods, lives on disciplined savings but occasionally splurges on colorful craft supplies; they balance imagination with a relaxed, sometimes disorganized lifestyle.
payload.cultural_background: Lalita Tirvadekar comes from a Konkani‑speaking Hindu community that settled in the rural hinterland of Jalgaon, Maharashtra. Her family maintains a blend of coastal Konkani customs—such as devotion to Ganesh and sharing of coconut‑based festive foods—and the agrarian traditions of interior Maharashtra, like local fairs (shashti), collective farming rituals and village temple gatherings. This cultural mix shapes her worldview, fostering a respect for community harmony and a love for folk arts.
payload.linguistic_background: Lalita grew up speaking Konkani at home and Marathi in the village and school setting. Though she is illiterate and cannot read or write in any language, she has strong oral communication skills. She can understand spoken Hindi due to everyday interactions and can follow work instructions in Konkani and Marathi. Her fluency lets her convey ideas creatively through storytelling and oral explanations, which aligns with her imaginative nature.
payload.skills_and_expertise: Lalita works as a mono line operator, handling installation, maintenance, and troubleshooting of single‑wire telephone lines in her rural area. She possesses manual dexterity, an ear for line quality, and the ability to interpret oral work instructions. Her job requires clear communication with villagers, so she has strong interpersonal and conflict‑resolution skills. Additionally, her creative side finds expression in folk art, embroidery, and improvisational storytelling, which she often uses to convey information in a memorable way.
payload.skills_and_expertise_list: ['mono line operation', 'basic electrical troubleshooting', 'manual dexterity', 'oral instruction following', 'community liaison', 'folk art creation', 'embroidery', 'conflict mediation']
payload.hobbies_and_interests: Lalita enjoys creating abstract paintings and colorful folk motifs on fabric and paper. She spends evenings listening to classical Hindustani music and practicing the lively Lavani dance steps during village celebrations. Gardening, especially growing seasonal vegetables and marigolds, gives her a sense of calm. She loves oral storytelling, sharing local legends with children, and attending weekly village fairs where she explores new crafts and ideas.
payload.hobbies_and_interests_list: ['abstract painting', 'folk motif creation', 'listening to Hindustani classical music', 'practicing Lavani dance', 'gardening', 'oral storytelling', 'attending village fairs', 'exploring new crafts']
payload.career_goals_and_ambitions: Lalita aspires to advance from being a mono line operator to a supervisory role within the telecom department, where she can mentor other workers. She plans to enroll in a government adult‑literacy program to gain basic reading and writing skills, which will enable her to handle records independently. In the longer term, she dreams of turning her talent for folk art and embroidery into a small home‑based handicraft business, providing supplemental income for her family and promoting cultural heritage.
payload.persona_clinical_summary: Lalita Tirvadekar is a 34‑year‑old female from Maharashtra’s Jalgaon district, currently married, with a Konkani first language and limited formal education. She works as a mono‑line operator in a rural telecom setting, is illiterate, and resides in a rural zone. Her lifestyle includes community‑oriented routines, occasional anxiety, and a preference for oral communication over written tasks.

payload.user_prompt:
Write one complete PHC Register Entry (Primary Health Centre) for clinical domain Maternal Health.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Konkani (code=kok, script=Devanagari). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Maternal Health — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
MATERNAL SAFETY: clinical content must be age-plausible for persona age=34. Routine ANC/delivery typically ages 18–40; ages 40–42 → high-risk ANC/PNC/contraception — never invent extreme late pregnancy as a routine story.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Maharashtra, district=Jalgaon.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Devanagari for Konkani.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Female
- age: 34
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: Mono Operator
- education_level: Illiterate
- zone: Rural
- state: Maharashtra
- district: Jalgaon
- first_language: Konkani
- clinical_persona_summary: Lalita Tirvadekar is a 34‑year‑old female from Maharashtra’s Jalgaon district, currently married, with a Konkani first language and limited formal education. She works as a mono‑line operator in a rural telecom setting, is illiterate, and resides in a rural zone. Her lifestyle includes community‑oriented routines, occasional anxiety, and a preference for oral communication over written tasks.

Document format guide for PHC Register Entry (Primary Health Centre) (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
PHC register — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|PHC ...]] Village [[VILLAGE|...]] District [[DISTRICT|...]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|...]] [[AGE|19]] [[GENDER|Female]] | brief entry line

MANDATORY inline entities (11 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- VILLAGE — Village e.g. [[VILLAGE|Shivpur]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- ASHA_WORKER_NAME — ASHA Worker Name e.g. [[ASHA_WORKER_NAME|Sunita Yadav]]
- BPL_RATION_CARD — BPL / Ration Card Number e.g. [[BPL_RATION_CARD|BPL-UP-208001-4421]]
- VOTER_ID — Voter ID e.g. [[VOTER_ID|ABC1234567]]
- ABHA_ID — ABHA ID e.g. [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.
payload.generated_text:
PHC register — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Primary Health Centre, Pachora]] Village [[VILLAGE|Pachora]] District [[DISTRICT|Jalgaon]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|Lalita Tirvadekar]] [[AGE|34]] [[GENDER|Female]] | Routine Antenatal Check-up

Patient presents for routine antenatal check-up. Last menstrual period was 8 weeks ago. First trimester scan done at private clinic. No complaints of bleeding, pain, or swelling. Contact number provided is [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]].

Vitals stable. BP 110/70 mmHg. Weight 65 kg. Uterine size corresponds to 10 weeks gestation. Fetal heart rate 140 bpm. No edema. Urine routine normal. Hemoglobin: 11.5 g/dL. Blood group: O positive. BPL card number [[BPL_RATION_CARD|BPL-MH-JLG-2024-00123]] noted for benefits. Voter ID [[VOTER_ID|MH1234567890]] and ABHA ID [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]] recorded in system.

Diagnosis: Antenatal care (ANC) - 10 weeks gestation.

Plan: Continue routine ANC. Prescribe folic acid 5 mg daily. Advise diet rich in iron and protein. Schedule next visit in 4 weeks. Counsel on danger signs. ASHA worker [[ASHA_WORKER_NAME|Sunita Patil]] to follow up at home.

payload.travel_persona: Jamal Shamar prefers short getaways to serene spots like Gulmarg and Pahalgam for photography and trekking, while dreaming of a cultural pilgrimage to Istanbul to explore historic Sufi shrines, balancing travel plans with modest savings and family responsibilities.
payload.culinary_persona: Jamal Shamar delights in sipping fragrant kahwa with almond slivers and cardamom after prayers, enjoys a hearty plate of rogan josh on weekends, and often treats themselves to street‑side kebabs, though they rarely cook at home.
payload.persona: Jamal Shamar is a detail‑obsessed keypunch specialist who balances meticulous work with a quiet love for poetry, Sufi music, and mountain sketches, while wrestling with anxiety and a habit of occasional impulse art‑supply splurges.
payload.cultural_background: Jamal belongs to the Kashmiri Muslim community of the Kashmir Valley, where the confluence of South Asian, Central Asian and Himalayan cultural traditions shapes daily life. He was raised in an urban household in Srinagar, where families still observe traditional Kashmiri rituals such as daily prayers, giving of 'Sukhni' during festivals, and the seasonal enjoyment of 'Kahwa' (green tea with almonds). The city's heritage of poetry, handicrafts, and the legacy of Sufi saints imbues his worldview with an appreciation for artistic expression, while his technical diploma introduced practical skills.
payload.linguistic_background: Jamal's mother tongue is Kashmiri, which he reads and writes fluently in the Perso‑Arabic script commonly used in the Kashmir Valley. His formal education was conducted in English, so he has functional proficiency in reading and writing English, essential for his role as a keypunch operator, though he is less comfortable speaking it. He also has basic literacy in Hindi, mainly recognizing Devanagari letters, but does not use it regularly. His linguistic repertoire reflects the multilingual environment of urban Srinagar.
payload.skills_and_expertise: Jamal’s core expertise lies in high‑speed data entry and punched‑card operation, requiring meticulous attention to detail and strict adherence to formats. He is proficient with legacy keypunch machines as well as modern computer‑based data entry software, including MS Excel and basic database tools. His organized nature translates into strong time‑management, workflow optimisation, and record‑keeping practices. Additionally, his curiosity fuels self‑learning in emerging digital tools, and his art appreciation endows him with an eye for visual detail, beneficial in quality‑control tasks.
payload.skills_and_expertise_list: ['Data Entry', 'Keypunch Machine Operation', 'Attention to Detail', 'Time Management', 'Record Keeping', 'Microsoft Excel', 'Basic Database Management', 'Self-Learning of Digital Tools', 'Art Appreciation']
payload.hobbies_and_interests: Jamal finds solace in solitary pursuits that blend his artistic curiosity with his love for nature. He enjoys reading Kashmiri poetry, especially verses of Amir Khusrau and contemporary poets, and often sketches the snow‑capped hills of the valley in a small sketchbook. He is an avid listener of Sufi music and occasionally plays the Rabab in informal gatherings. Outdoor activities like trekking along the Dal Lake promenade and photographing the sunrise over the Zabarwan range are favorite pastimes. To manage stress, he practices simple breathing exercises and occasional yoga, aligning with his disciplined routine. He also experiments with digital tools to create a personal archive of his favorite artwork and literary excerpts.
payload.hobbies_and_interests_list: ['Reading Kashmiri poetry', 'Sketching landscapes', 'Listening to Sufi music', 'Playing the Rabab', 'Trekking around Dal Lake', 'Sunrise photography', 'Breathing exercises', 'Yoga', 'Digital archiving of art and literature']
payload.career_goals_and_ambitions: Jamal aims to transition from manual keypunch work to a supervisory role in digital data management within the next three to five years. He plans to acquire certifications in database administration and data analytics, leveraging his meticulous nature to lead a team that handles large‑scale digitisation projects of governmental records, especially those related to Kashmiri heritage. He also aspires to integrate his love for art by developing a digital archive of Kashmiri poetry and crafts, positioning himself as a bridge between tradition and technology. Financially, he hopes to achieve a stable income that ensures his family's comfort and supports his wife's entrepreneurship aspirations.

payload.system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Use ONLY allow-list TYPE names. Billing aliases: EMAIL/CONTACT_EMAIL → EMAIL_ADDRESS; ADDRESS_NUMBER → RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS; RECEIPT_NUMBER and STATE are NOT types (receipt no. / state stay plain prose; geography uses DISTRICT).

- PAN must match ^[A-Z]{5}[0-9]{4}[A-Z]$ e.g. ABCDE1234F — not KAAPR1234C-style junk.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Maternal_health must be age-plausible: routine ANC/delivery for ages ~18–40; ages 40–42 → high-risk ANC / PNC / contraception — never invent extreme late pregnancy (e.g. 49yo as routine 28-week gestation).

- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Format examples (copy the bracket pattern; invent new synthetic values):
  [[AADHAAR_NUMBER|157815659387]]
  [[ABHA_ADDRESS|meena.devi@abdm]]
  [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]

  [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]]

  [[BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER|50200012345678]]

  [[BPL_RATION_CARD|BPL-UP-208001-4421]]

  [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
  [[DRIVING_LICENCE|UP14 20110012345]]

  [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-77821]]

  [[GENDER|Female]]
  [[HOSPITAL_ID|HSP-UP-014]]
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
  [[IFSC_CODE|SBIN0001234]]
  [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]
  [[INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER|POL-PB-2024-7781]]
  [[IP_ADDRESS|103.21.244.12]]
  [[MAC_ADDRESS|00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E]]

  [[OCCUPATION|Teacher]]
  [[PAN_NUMBER|ABCDE1234F]]

  [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
  [[RELIGION|Hindu]]
  [[RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS|12 Gandhi Nagar, Ward 4]]
  [[STUDENT_ID|STU-2024-0912]]
  [[TAX_ID|29ABCDE1234F1Z5]]
  [[TELEPHONE_LANDLINE|0512-2345678]]
  [[URL|https://example.org/u/meena]]
  [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|UP78AB1234]]
  [[VILLAGE|Shivpur]]
  [[VOTER_ID|ABC1234567]]
  [[WARD_NUMBER|B2]]

Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content;
  geriatric / extreme-age routine 28-week pregnancy.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; maternal content is
  age-plausible (no unsafe extreme late pregnancy as a routine story).

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs (billing: EMAIL→EMAIL_ADDRESS, ADDRESS→RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS;
  receipt/state/money stay plain prose). Valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.

Write one complete Telemedicine / Doctor-Patient Chat Transcript for clinical domain General Medicine.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Kashmiri (code=ks, script=Arabic). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.

ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.

GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Jammu & Kashmir, district=Srinagar.

payload.generated_text:
--- session ---
Patient [[PATIENT_NAME|Jammer Shamar]] [[AGE|28]] [[GENDER|Male]]
Appt [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-2024-0815-001]] Portal [[URL|https://tele.example.in/visit]]
Client IP [[IP_ADDRESS|103.21.244.12]] Device IMEI [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]
MAC [[MAC_ADDRESS|00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E]] Phone [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
Email [[EMAIL_ADDRESS|jammer.shamar@example.com]] Hospital [[HOSPITAL_NAME|SKIMS Soura]]
--- chat ---
Patient: Namaste doctor. I have been experiencing persistent headaches for the past three days. The pain is mainly in the frontal region and gets worse when I am at my workplace operating the keypunch machine. I also feel some dizziness and nausea in the mornings.

Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Ayesha Qazi]]: Hello Jammer. I understand your concern. Can you describe the nature of the headache? Is it throbbing, sharp, or dull? Also, have you noticed any visual disturbances or sensitivity to light?

Patient: The pain is more like a dull ache that sometimes becomes sharp. I haven't noticed any visual changes, but bright lights do bother me a bit. I've been taking paracetamol which gives temporary relief. I also feel tired easily and have been sleeping poorly due to the pain.

Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Ayesha Qazi]]: Thank you for the details. Given your symptoms of frontal headache with nausea and dizziness, I'm concerned about possible tension headaches or migraine variant. Have you experienced any recent stress at work or home? Also, do you have any history of similar headaches?

Patient: Actually, I've been quite stressed at work with a tight deadline for a new project. My wife mentioned I seem more anxious lately. This is the first time I'm experiencing such severe headaches. I don't have any history of migraines in my family.

Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Ayesha Qazi]]: I see. The stress could definitely be triggering these headaches. I'd like to recommend some immediate measures. Please try to take regular breaks from your keypunch work, every 30 minutes for 5 minutes. Also, ensure proper hydration and avoid skipping meals. For the pain, continue paracetamol but avoid exceeding 3 grams per day.

Patient: Thank you doctor. What about long-term management? I'm worried this might continue.

Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Ayesha Qazi]]: For long-term prevention, I suggest you start a simple headache diary to track triggers. Also, consider starting a low-dose prophylactic medication like amitriptyline 10mg at night, which can help with both headaches and anxiety. We can reassess in two weeks. If symptoms worsen or you develop fever, vomiting, or neck stiffness, please visit the emergency department immediately.

Patient: I'll start the diary and try the medication. Should I continue with my regular work?

Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Ayesha Qazi]]: Yes, but with modifications as I mentioned. If the headaches become severe and prevent you from working, take a day off to rest. Also, practice deep breathing exercises during breaks. Remember to maintain good posture while working at the keypunch machine.

Patient: One more question doctor - is this related to my anxiety?

Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Ayesha Qazi]]: Yes, there's a strong connection between anxiety and tension headaches. Managing your anxiety through regular exercise, meditation, or counseling can significantly reduce headache frequency. Since you mentioned enjoying poetry and music, perhaps incorporating these activities as relaxation techniques could help.

Patient: That's helpful advice. I'll try to incorporate these changes. Thank you for your time, doctor.

Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Ayesha Qazi]]: You're welcome, Jammer. Please don't hesitate to contact us if you have any concerns. We'll schedule a follow-up appointment in two weeks. Take care and take those breaks at work!

payload.skills_and_expertise: Jamal’s core expertise lies in high‑speed data entry and punched‑card operation, requiring meticulous attention to detail and strict adherence to formats. He is proficient with legacy keypunch machines as well as modern computer‑based data entry software, including MS Excel and basic database tools. His organized nature translates into strong time‑management, workflow optimisation, and record‑keeping practices. Additionally, his curiosity fuels self‑learning in emerging digital tools, and his art appreciation endows him with an eye for visual detail, beneficial in quality‑control tasks.

payload.system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Use ONLY allow-list TYPE names. Billing aliases: EMAIL/CONTACT_EMAIL → EMAIL_ADDRESS; ADDRESS_NUMBER → RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS; RECEIPT_NUMBER and STATE are NOT types (receipt no. / state stay plain prose; geography uses DISTRICT).

- PAN must match ^[A-Z]{5}[0-9]{4}[A-Z]$ e.g. ABCDE1234F — not KAAPR1234C-style junk.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Maternal_health must be age-plausible: routine ANC/delivery for ages ~18–40; ages 40–42 → high-risk ANC / PNC / contraception — never invent extreme late pregnancy (e.g. 49yo as routine 28-week gestation).

- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Format examples (copy the bracket pattern; invent new synthetic values):
  [[AADHAAR_NUMBER|157815659387]]
  [[ABHA_ADDRESS|meena.devi@abdm]]
  [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]
  [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-0912]]
  [[AGE|34]]
  [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]]
  [[ASHA_WORKER_NAME|Sunita Yadav]]
  [[BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER|50200012345678]]
  [[BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER|SBIN0001234]]
  [[BED_NUMBER|12]]
  [[BPL_RATION_CARD|BPL-UP-208001-4421]]
  [[CASTE|OBC]]
  [[CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER|4111111111111111]]
  [[CVV|123]]
  [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
  [[DOB|1992-03-14]]
  [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
  [[DRIVING_LICENCE|UP14 20110012345]]
  [[EMAIL_ADDRESS|meena.devi@example.com]]
  [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-77821]]
  [[ENCOUNTER_ID|ENC-55601]]
  [[GENDER|Female]]
  [[HOSPITAL_ID|HSP-UP-014]]
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
  [[IFSC_CODE|SBIN0001234]]
  [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]
  [[INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER|POL-PB-2024-7781]]
  [[IP_ADDRESS|103.21.244.12]]
  [[MAC_ADDRESS|00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E]]
  [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
  [[OCCUPATION|Teacher]]
  [[PAN_NUMBER|ABCDE1234F]]
  [[PASSPORT_NUMBER|Z1234567]]
  [[PATIENT_ID|PID-77821]]
  [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
  [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
  [[PIN|4829]]
  [[PIN_CODE|208001]]
  [[REFERRAL_ID|REF-2024-1123]]
  [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
  [[RELIGION|Hindu]]
  [[RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS|12 Gandhi Nagar, Ward 4]]
  [[STUDENT_ID|STU-2024-0912]]
  [[TAX_ID|29ABCDE1234F1Z5]]
  [[TELEPHONE_LANDLINE|0512-2345678]]
  [[URL|https://example.org/u/meena]]
  [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|UP78AB1234]]
  [[VILLAGE|Shivpur]]
  [[VOTER_ID|ABC1234567]]
  [[WARD_NUMBER|B2]]

Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content;
  geriatric / extreme-age routine 28-week pregnancy.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; maternal content is
  age-plausible (no unsafe extreme late pregnancy as a routine story).

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs (billing: EMAIL→EMAIL_ADDRESS, ADDRESS→RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS;
  receipt/state/money stay plain prose). Valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
payload.user_prompt:
Write one complete OPD Slip (Outpatient Department) for clinical domain Tuberculosis & Non-Communicable Diseases.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Kashmiri (code=ks, script=Arabic). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Tuberculosis & Non-Communicable Diseases — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Jammu & Kashmir, district=Srinagar.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Arabic for Kashmiri.

PLACEMENT (OPD header): First line MUST include BOTH [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] (facility name) AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] (facility code) — never substitute one for the other.
Put [[EMPLOYEE_ID|…]] on the registrar/clerk line and [[RELATIVE_NAME|…]] on the attendant/relative line. Include [[DOB|YYYY-MM-DD]] and [[OCCUPATION|…]] with demographics.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Male
- age: 28
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: Keypunch Operator
- education_level: Technical diploma or certificate not equal to degree
- zone: Urban
- state: Jammu & Kashmir
- district: Srinagar
- first_language: Kashmiri
- clinical_persona_summary: Jammer Shamar, a 28-year-old male, is currently married and works as a keypunch operator in an urban Srinagar setting. He holds a technical diploma and speaks Kashmiri as his first language, with functional English proficiency. His lifestyle includes a quiet love for poetry and Sufi music, and he manages anxiety while maintaining a disciplined workflow.

Document format guide for OPD Slip (Outpatient Department) (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton (adapt language/script; keep tags):
OPD Slip | [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital X]] | ID [[HOSPITAL_ID|DH-X-01]]
Patient: [[PATIENT_NAME|...]] | DOB [[DOB|1992-03-14]] | Age: [[AGE|34]] | Gender: [[GENDER|Female]]
Occupation: [[OCCUPATION|Teacher]] | MRN: [[MRN|OPD-2024-001]] | Doctor: [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. ...]]
Relative: [[RELATIVE_NAME|...]] | Phone: [[PHONE_NUMBER|98XXXXXXXX]]
Registrar EmpID: [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-4412]] | District: [[DISTRICT|...]]
Chief complaint: <short clinical line matching assigned domain>

MANDATORY inline entities (13 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- DOB — Date of Birth e.g. [[DOB|1992-03-14]]
- OCCUPATION — Occupation e.g. [[OCCUPATION|Teacher]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- HOSPITAL_ID — Hospital ID e.g. [[HOSPITAL_ID|HSP-UP-014]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- RELATIVE_NAME — Relative Name (Father/Mother/Spouse/Guardian) e.g. [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
- EMPLOYEE_ID — Employee ID e.g. [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-77821]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.
payload.generated_text:
OPD Slip | [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Sher-i-Kashmir Institute of Medical Sciences]] | ID [[HOSPITAL_ID|SKIMS-SGR-001]]
Patient: [[PATIENT_NAME|Jammer Shamar]] | DOB [[DOB|1996-05-15]] | Age: [[AGE|28]] | Gender: [[GENDER|Male]]
Occupation: [[OCCUPATION|Keypunch Operator]] | MRN: [[MRN|OPD-2024-0892-001]] | Doctor: [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Nayeem Ahmad]]
Relative: [[RELATIVE_NAME|Aftab Ahmad]] | Phone: [[PHONE_NUMBER|9419012345]]
Registrar EmpID: [[EMPLOYEE_ID|REG-2024-0892]] | District: [[DISTRICT|Srinagar]]
Chief complaint: Persistent dry cough for 3 weeks with occasional blood-tinged sputum, low-grade evening fever, and 5kg weight loss. Patient also reports mild breathlessness on exertion and occasional chest discomfort.

History of Present Illness: Patient is a 28-year-old male keypunch operator presenting with progressive cough for 3 weeks. Initially dry cough, now productive with occasional blood-tinged sputum. Associated symptoms include evening low-grade fever (99-100°F), night sweats, and significant weight loss of 5kg over 2 months. Reports mild dyspnea on climbing stairs. No known sick contacts.

Past Medical History: Diagnosed with hypertension 6 months ago, on regular medication. No known diabetes or cardiac disease. No previous TB exposure or treatment.

Physical Examination:
- General: Thin build, conscious, oriented, mildly anxious
- Vital signs: BP 140/90 mmHg, Pulse 88/min, Temp 99.2°F, RR 18/min, SpO2 94% on room air
- Chest: Bilateral breath sounds present, occasional rhonchi in right upper zone, no wheezing or crepitations
- Other systems: Within normal limits

Investigations Ordered:
- Sputum for AFB smear and culture
- Chest X-ray PA view
- Complete blood count
- ESR and CRP
- Blood sugar fasting and post-prandial
- ECG

Provisional Diagnosis:
1. Pulmonary Tuberculosis (RNTCP Category I)
2. Essential Hypertension

Treatment Plan:
- Start anti-tubercular therapy as per RNTCP guidelines
- Continue antihypertensive medication
- Nutritional supplementation
- Follow-up in 2 weeks for review of investigations and treatment response

Patient counseled regarding TB treatment adherence, importance of completing full course, and side effects monitoring. Advised to report any adverse effects immediately.

Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content;
  geriatric / extreme-age routine 28-week pregnancy.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; maternal content is
  age-plausible (no unsafe extreme late pregnancy as a routine story).

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs (billing: EMAIL→EMAIL_ADDRESS, ADDRESS→RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS;
  receipt/state/money stay plain prose). Valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
payload.user_prompt:
Write one complete ER Triage & Nursing Notes for clinical domain Psychiatry / Behavioral Health.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Kashmiri (code=ks, script=Arabic). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Psychiatry / Behavioral Health — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Jammu & Kashmir, district=Srinagar.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Arabic for Kashmiri.

PLACEMENT (vehicle): Put VEHICLE_REGISTRATION exactly once in the arrival/RTA/ambulance line. Do not dump or repeat it.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Male
- age: 19
- marital_status: Never Married
- occupation: No Occupation / Retired / Homemaker
- education_level: Higher Secondary/Intermediate Pre-University/Senior Secondary
- zone: Urban
- state: Jammu & Kashmir
- district: Srinagar
- first_language: Kashmiri
- clinical_persona_summary: A 19‑year‑old male from Srinagar, Jammu & Kashmir, who is unmarried and retired, has completed higher secondary education. He is fluent in Kashmiri and has functional English proficiency. His lifestyle includes a passion for visual arts, digital storytelling, and community heritage activities.

Document format guide for ER Triage & Nursing Notes (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
ER triage — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]] Encounter [[ENCOUNTER_ID|...]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|...]] [[AGE|22]] [[GENDER|Male]] Ward [[WARD_NUMBER|ER]] Bed [[BED_NUMBER|...]]
Arrived by ambulance / RTA vehicle [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|PB10AB1234]] (exactly once).
Relative [[RELATIVE_NAME|...]] Phone [[PHONE_NUMBER|...]] Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|...]]
Vitals / acuity: <short notes>

MANDATORY inline entities (11 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- ENCOUNTER_ID — Encounter / Visit ID e.g. [[ENCOUNTER_ID|ENC-55601]]
- WARD_NUMBER — Ward Number e.g. [[WARD_NUMBER|B2]]
- RELATIVE_NAME — Relative Name (Father/Mother/Spouse/Guardian) e.g. [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- BED_NUMBER — Bed Number e.g. [[BED_NUMBER|12]]
- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION — Vehicle Registration Number e.g. [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|UP78AB1234]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.
payload.generated_text:
ER triage — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|SMHS Hospital]] Encounter [[ENCOUNTER_ID|ENC-2024-0315-001]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|Imran Ahmad]] [[AGE|19]] [[GENDER|Male]] Ward [[WARD_NUMBER|ER]] Bed [[BED_NUMBER|07]]
Arrived by ambulance [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|JK01AB1234]]
Relative [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ghulam Ahmad]] Phone [[PHONE_NUMBER|9419012345]] Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Farooq Qureshi]]
Vitals / acuity: Patient presents with acute anxiety episode, elevated heart rate 110 bpm, BP 140/90, mild agitation. Appears oriented x3 but reports intrusive thoughts and auditory hallucinations. No acute medical distress noted.

Nursing assessment: Patient is a 19-year-old Kashmiri male, unmarried, unemployed, brought by father for sudden onset of distressing thoughts and hearing voices. Reports decreased sleep over past 3 days, increased irritability, and social withdrawal. Denies suicidal ideation but expresses hopelessness about future. Patient appears anxious, pacing in room, hands trembling. Mother reports similar episodes in past, patient on no regular medications.

Plan: Admit to psychiatric ward for observation and evaluation. Initiate low-dose anxiolytic as needed. Schedule psychiatric consultation within 2 hours. Monitor vitals q4h. Provide quiet environment, minimal stimulation. Family education provided on crisis intervention.

payload.professional_persona: Sha Shamar is an aspiring visual storyteller who blends sketching, photography, and digital design, using Canva and Photoshop to craft vibrant social media content, while they maintain a relaxed, flexible mindset that lets them juggle projects without rigid deadlines.
payload.linguistic_persona: Sha Shamar is fluent in their native language, comfortably writing in its Perso‑Arabic script, speaks functional English for schoolwork and social media, and they pick up bits of Hindi and Urdu from songs and movies despite limited practice.
payload.sports_persona: Sha Shamar enjoys weekend cricket matches with friends, cheers for the Mumbai Indians in IPL, and occasionally jogs along the lake promenade for quick cardio, but they prefer casual play over regimented gym routines.
payload.arts_persona: Sha Shamar is a passionate sketcher and budding digital photographer who curates eclectic Spotify playlists featuring Nusrat Fateh Ali Khan, Kailash Kher and the regional folk band Baal, while they regularly capture street art for Instagram and draw inspiration from Sufi poetry and contemporary Indian poet Gulzar.
payload.travel_persona: Sha Shamar loves spontaneous short treks to nearby hill stations like Gulmarg and Pahalgam, plans trips on a shoestring budget using trains and shared rides, dreams of exploring Delhi’s museums and Jaipur’s heritage forts, and they often capture travel moments on their phone for Instagram stories.
payload.culinary_persona: Sha Shamar delights in the rich flavours of Rogan Josh, Dum Aloo, Haakh and fragrant Kahwa, they frequently sample family‑made recipes while rarely cooking personally, preferring to savor street‑food stalls that serve gurgi‑baked naan and saffron‑spiced gushtaba.
payload.persona: Sha Shamar is a 19‑year‑old visual arts enthusiast who blends sketching, photography, and Sufi vibes into digital storytelling, they manage quirky procrastination with a heart for community heritage.
payload.cultural_background: Sha Shamar hails from the vibrant Kashmiri Muslim community of Srinagar, an urban centre in the Kashmir Valley. His family follows the cultural traditions of the valley – from attending the spring fairs (Urs) and listening to Sufi poetry (Shab-e-Mir), to enjoying traditional Kashmiri cuisine like Rogan Josh and Kahwa. The city’s rich heritage of wooden handicrafts, papier‑mâché art, and Pashmina weaving forms part of his everyday surroundings. Growing up in a close‑knit neighbourhood, he has been exposed to a blend of modern urban life and the age‑old customs that celebrate hospitality, community gatherings and respect for nature.
payload.linguistic_background: He is fluent in Kashmiri, both spoken and written, comfortable with the Perso‑Arabic script used for the language. Through his schooling, he has functional proficiency in English – can understand textbooks, converse at a basic level and write simple essays. He has limited exposure to Hindi and Urdu, mainly through media, but does not consider them as fluent languages.
payload.skills_and_expertise: Sha shows a natural flair for visual arts, especially sketching and digital photography. He is proficient with basic computer operations and uses graphic design tools like Canva and Photoshop for creating social media content. His command of Kashmiri calligraphy allows him to produce stylised text for local newsletters. Additionally, he has a good understanding of Kashmiri folk crafts, enabling him to assist family members in Pashmina maintenance and craft‑selling decisions. He is also adept at organizing informal study groups among peers, reflecting his cooperative nature.
payload.skills_and_expertise_list: ["Sketching", "Digital photography", "Basic computer operation", "Graphic design (Canva, Photoshop)", "Kashmiri calligraphy", "Knowledge of Kashmiri folk crafts", "Organizing study groups"]
payload.hobbies_and_interests: He enjoys sketching urban landscapes of Srinagar, listening to Sufi qawwali, reading contemporary poetry, exploring the surrounding hills for short treks, playing cricket with friends on weekends, experimenting with traditional Kashmiri recipes such as Dum Aloo and haakh, and curating playlists on Spotify that blend world music with local folk tunes. He also likes capturing street art on his phone and sharing it on Instagram.
payload.hobbies_and_interests_list: ["Sketching urban landscapes", "Listening to Sufi qawwali", "Reading contemporary poetry", "Short treks in the hills", "Playing cricket", "Cooking traditional Kashmiri dishes", "Curating music playlists", "Street photography and Instagram sharing"]
payload.career_goals_and_ambitions: Sha aims to enroll in a bachelor’s programme in Visual Arts or Media Studies at a reputable university, preferably in Delhi or Jammu. He aspires to become a multimedia storyteller who documents the rich cultural tapestry of Kashmir through photography, video, and digital blogs. In the long term, he envisions launching an online platform that showcases Kashmiri artisans, promotes ethical tourism, and provides training for youth in digital content creation. He is keen on gaining internships with media houses and NGOs that work on heritage conservation, hoping to blend his artistic talent with social impact.
payload.persona_clinical_summary: A 19‑year‑old male from Srinagar, Jammu & Kashmir, who is unmarried and retired, has completed higher secondary education. He is fluent in Kashmiri and has functional English proficiency. His lifestyle includes a passion for visual arts, digital storytelling, and community heritage activities.

NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Arabic for Kashmiri.

- sex / gender: Male
- age: 19
- marital_status: Never Married
- occupation: No Occupation / Retired / Homemaker
- education_level: Higher Secondary/Intermediate Pre-University/Senior Secondary
- zone: Urban
- state: Jammu & Kashmir
- district: Srinagar
- first_language: Kashmiri
- clinical_persona_summary: A 19‑year‑old male from Srinagar, Jammu & Kashmir, who is unmarried and retired, has completed higher secondary education. He is fluent in Kashmiri and has functional English proficiency. His lifestyle includes a passion for visual arts, digital storytelling, and community heritage activities.

Document format guide for Telemedicine / Doctor-Patient Chat Transcript (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):

session header (once each), then keep chat turns free of ID/metadata spam:
--- session ---
Patient [[PATIENT_NAME|...]] [[AGE|34]] [[GENDER|Female]]
Appt [[APPOINTMENT_ID|...]] Portal [[URL|https://tele.example.in/visit]]
Client IP [[IP_ADDRESS|103.21.244.12]] Device IMEI [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]
MAC [[MAC_ADDRESS|A1:B2:C3:D4:E5:F6]] Phone [[PHONE_NUMBER|98XXXXXXXX]]
Email [[EMAIL_ADDRESS|...]] Hospital [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]]
--- chat ---
Patient: <symptom turn — no AGE/GENDER/PHONE re-tags>
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|...]]: <advice turn>

- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- EMAIL_ADDRESS — Email Address e.g. [[EMAIL_ADDRESS|meena.devi@example.com]]
- APPOINTMENT_ID — Appointment ID e.g. [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]]
- IP_ADDRESS — IP Address e.g. [[IP_ADDRESS|103.21.244.12]]
- URL — URL (Personal Website/Profile) e.g. [[URL|https://example.org/u/meena]]

- MAC_ADDRESS — MAC Address e.g. [[MAC_ADDRESS|00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E]]

payload.generated_text:
--- session ---
Patient [[PATIENT_NAME|Aamir Khan]] [[AGE|19]] [[GENDER|Male]]
Appt [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-2024-0315-001]] Portal [[URL|https://tele.example.in/visit]]
Client IP [[IP_ADDRESS|103.21.244.12]] Device IMEI [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]
MAC [[MAC_ADDRESS|00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E]] Phone [[PHONE_NUMBER|9419876543]]
Email [[EMAIL_ADDRESS|aamir.khan@example.com]] Hospital [[HOSPITAL_NAME|SMHS Hospital]]
--- chat ---
Patient: Doctor sahab, I have been having persistent cough for the past three weeks, along with fever in the evenings and some weight loss. I also feel tired all the time.
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Riyaz Ahmad]]: Aamir, I understand your concern. These symptoms could indicate tuberculosis. Have you been exposed to anyone with TB recently? Do you have any other medical conditions like diabetes?
Patient: No, I don't have diabetes. But I do have some breathing difficulties when I climb stairs. My father had TB when I was young, but he was treated successfully.
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Riyaz Ahmad]]: Given your family history and symptoms, we need to conduct some tests. Please visit SMHS Hospital tomorrow for a sputum test and chest X-ray. I will also prescribe some initial medications while we wait for the results.
Patient: Thank you, doctor. What medications should I start with?
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Riyaz Ahmad]]: I'm prescribing Isoniazid, Rifampicin, Pyrazinamide, and Ethambutol for four months. Please take them daily as prescribed. Also, maintain good nutrition and rest. Avoid smoking and alcohol during treatment.
Patient: I don't smoke or drink, but I'm worried about the cost of treatment.
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Riyaz Ahmad]]: Don't worry about the cost. The government provides free TB treatment at SMHS Hospital. You just need to bring your Aadhaar card and any previous medical records. We also have a patient support system to help with transportation if needed.
Patient: That's a relief. When should I come for the tests?
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Riyaz Ahmad]]: Please come tomorrow between 9 AM to 11 AM. Take your [[PHONE_NUMBER|9419876543]] with you for registration. If you have any questions before your appointment, you can call me at the hospital or email me at [[EMAIL_ADDRESS|aamir.khan@example.com]].
Patient: Thank you, doctor. I will be there tomorrow.
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Riyaz Ahmad]]: You're welcome, Aamir. Remember, TB is completely curable with proper treatment. Take care and get well soon.

payload.persona: Devanti Kumari is a diligent 23‑year‑old jewellery supervisor whose structured work ethic and vibrant social spirit are tempered by frequent anxiety and a dream of leading her own craft studio.
payload.cultural_background: Devanti comes from a Maithili‑speaking family in the rural hinterland of Saharsa, Bihar. Her upbringing is rooted in the agrarian culture of the Mithila region, where festivals such as Chhath, Jitiya and Saawan are celebrated with elaborate rituals. The local community places high value on family cohesion, respect for elders, and the preservation of traditional arts like Madhubani painting and handmade jewellery. Growing up in a joint‑family setting, she learned the importance of mutual support, hospitality and communal gatherings.
payload.linguistic_background: Devanti is a native speaker of Maithili and uses it as her primary means of communication at home and in the community. Although she cannot read or write due to her illiteracy, she has acquired basic comprehension of spoken Hindi through daily interactions and media such as radio. She does not have proficiency in English or any other language.
payload.skills_and_expertise: Devanti has developed strong supervisory skills in the jewellery and precision metal‑working sector. She coordinates daily production schedules, ensures that engraving patterns meet design specifications, and conducts quality checks on finished pieces. Her hands‑on experience with tools such as hand‑drills, gravers and polishing machines gives her practical expertise in metal shaping and fine detailing. Additionally, she manages a small team of artisans, handling labour allocation, conflict resolution and on‑the‑spot training. Her keen eye for market trends helps her adapt designs to customer preferences in the local market.
