GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content;
  geriatric / extreme-age routine 28-week pregnancy.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; maternal content is
  age-plausible (no unsafe extreme late pregnancy as a routine story).

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs (billing: EMAIL→EMAIL_ADDRESS, ADDRESS→RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS;
  receipt/state/money stay plain prose). Valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
payload.user_prompt:
Write one complete Prescription for clinical domain General Medicine.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Punjabi (code=pa, script=Gurmukhi). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match General Medicine — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Punjab, district=Moga.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Gurmukhi for Punjabi.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Male
- age: 18
- marital_status: Never Married
- occupation: Stall and Market Salespersons, Other
- education_level: Matric/Secondary
- zone: Urban
- state: Punjab
- district: Moga
- first_language: Punjabi
- clinical_persona_summary: A pragmatic 18‑year‑old male from Moga, Punjab, works as a market salesperson with a matriculation education and lives in an urban setting. He is never married, speaks Punjabi as his first language, and has functional Hindi proficiency with limited English. His lifestyle includes late‑night comic reading and a love for Bhangra music, while he saves money from his stall earnings.

Document format guide for Prescription (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
Prescription — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]]
Patient [[PATIENT_NAME|...]], [[AGE|28]] / [[GENDER|Male]], MRN [[MRN|RX-1001]]
Dr. [[DOCTOR_NAME|...]]
Rx: <drug dose frequency>  (do NOT tag drug names as entities)

MANDATORY inline entities (11 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS — Residential Address e.g. [[RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS|12 Gandhi Nagar, Ward 4]]
- PATIENT_ID — Patient ID e.g. [[PATIENT_ID|PID-77821]]
- ABHA_ID — ABHA ID e.g. [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.
payload.generated_text:
Prescription — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Civil Hospital Moga]]
Patient [[PATIENT_NAME|Gurpreet Singh]], [[AGE|18]] / [[GENDER|Male]], MRN [[MRN|CHM-2024-0012]]
Dr. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Rajinder Kumar]]
Phone: [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]]
Address: [[RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS|House No. 45, Urban Colony, Moga]]
ABHA ID: [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]
Patient ID: [[PATIENT_ID|PID-MGH-2024-0012]]
District: [[DISTRICT|Moga]]

Date: 15 May 2024

Patient presents with fever, cough, and mild body ache for 3 days. Temperature 101.2°F, mild pharyngitis noted. Prescribed:
- Paracetamol 500mg tablet, 1 tablet every 6 hours as needed for fever
- Azithromycin 250mg tablet, 1 tablet daily for 3 days
- Steam inhalation twice daily
- Plenty of fluids and rest

Follow up in 3 days if symptoms persist. Avoid cold drinks and heavy meals.

Dr. Rajinder Kumar
MBBS, MD (Medicine)
Civil Hospital Moga

payload.professional_persona: Neelam Bala, a 29‑year‑old aspiring home‑based tiffin caterer, combines rigorous household budgeting, precise meal planning and rudimentary spreadsheet skills to deliver punctual, high‑quality regional meals, and they competitively build a support network of fellow homemakers.
payload.linguistic_persona: Neelam Bala speaks Punjabi at home using Gurmukhi, reads and writes Hindi in Devanagari for community dealings, and possesses just enough English to recognize product labels, though they remain limited to simple sentences.
payload.sports_persona: Neelam Bala maintains fitness through daily morning walks and yoga, follows the Punjab Kings in the IPL and enjoys watching local kabaddi tournaments, while they occasionally join a community cricket viewing club with friends.
payload.arts_persona: Neelam Bala relaxes by stitching Phulkari embroidery, reads Punjabi short stories by Amrita Pritam, and listens to folk icons like Gurdas Maan and Harbhajan Mann, while they occasionally hum bhajans during chores.
payload.travel_persona: Neelam Bala prefers well‑planned short trips to nearby hill stations like Shimla and Manali or cultural visits to Amritsar’s Golden Temple, balancing adventure with budget constraints and they often travel with a close circle of family friends.
payload.culinary_persona: Neelam Bala’s palate favors authentic regional flavors, regularly preparing sarson da saag, makki di roti, gur‑laden laddoos and herb‑infused tea with mint and coriander, while they enjoy experimenting with seasonal vegetables from their balcony garden.
payload.persona: Neelam Bala blends disciplined household management with a competitive streak, balancing meticulous budgeting and cooking while they occasionally worry‑driven over‑thinking.
payload.cultural_background: Neelam Bala belongs to the Punjabi Hindu community of Jalandhar. Growing up in an urban but tightly‑knit neighbourhood, she has been immersed in the vibrant customs of Punjab – from festive celebrations like Lohri and Diwali to the warmth of joint‑family gatherings. Her cultural upbringing emphasizes respect for elders, a love for Punjabi folk music and dance, and a strong sense of duty towards the household. These influences, combined with the practical, no‑nonsense ethos of city life, shape her preference for orderly routines, dependable habits, and a focus on tangible outcomes.
payload.linguistic_background: Neelam’s mother tongue is Punjabi, spoken in the Gurmukhi script, and she uses it for daily communication with family and neighbours. She is also fluent in Hindi, which she reads and writes using Devanagari, mainly for interacting with the wider community and accessing basic services. Her formal schooling ended before primary level, so her literacy is limited to simple reading and writing in both languages, and she has only a rudimentary grasp of English, sufficient for recognizing common words on product labels.
payload.skills_and_expertise: Neelam is adept at managing a household efficiently, budgeting monthly expenses, and planning meals for her family. She excels in preparing authentic Punjabi dishes such as sarson da saag, makki di roti, and various tiffin items. Her craftsmanship includes traditional Phulkari embroidery and basic sewing, which she occasionally uses for repairing clothing. Despite limited formal schooling, she possesses basic literacy in Punjabi (Gurmukhi) and Hindi (Devanagari) and can handle simple correspondence. She also has rudimentary computer skills, enough to use a basic spreadsheet for tracking household finances.
payload.skills_and_expertise_list: ["Household budgeting", "Punjabi cooking (authentic dishes)", "Traditional Phulkari embroidery", "Basic sewing and mending", "Simple record‑keeping with spreadsheets", "Basic literacy in Punjabi and Hindi", "Organising family events"]
payload.hobbies_and_interests: Neelam enjoys reading Punjabi short stories and poetry, tending to a small balcony garden where she grows herbs and seasonal flowers, experimenting with traditional Punjabi recipes, especially making sweets like gur and laddoo. She practices basic embroidery (Phulkari) as a calming activity and listens to bhajans and kirtans. To manage stress, she does light yoga and meditation. Occasionally, she joins a close‑knit group of friends for chai and gossip, but generally prefers solitary or small‑group activities.
payload.hobbies_and_interests_list: ["Reading Punjabi literature", "Gardening", "Traditional cooking", "Embroidery (Phulkari)", "Listening to devotional music", "Yoga and meditation"]
payload.career_goals_and_ambitions: Neelam's primary career ambition is to transform her household management expertise into a modest home‑based catering service that provides tiffin meals to local office workers. She seeks to complete an adult literacy certification to sharpen her reading, writing, and numeracy, and to acquire basic computer training for efficient bookkeeping. Within the next three to five years, she aims to be recognized for her punctual, high‑quality Punjabi cuisine, expand her client base, and contribute financially to her ageing parents. She also hopes to develop a small network of fellow homemakers for mutual support, reflecting her competitive drive to excel in a niche market.
payload.persona_clinical_summary: A 29-year-old female from Jalandhar, Punjab, who is never married, has below-primary education, and lives in an urban zone. She is a homemaker fluent in Punjabi and Hindi, managing household duties with disciplined budgeting and occasional over-thinking. Her lifestyle includes daily walks and yoga, and she follows Punjabi cultural customs like Lohri and Diwali.

payload.professional_persona: Neelam Bala, a 29‑year‑old aspiring home‑based tiffin caterer, combines rigorous household budgeting, precise meal planning and rudimentary spreadsheet skills to deliver punctual, high‑quality regional meals, and they competitively build a support network of fellow homemakers.

payload.arts_persona: Neelam Bala relaxes by stitching Phulkari embroidery, reads Punjabi short stories by Amrita Pritam, and listens to folk icons like Gurdas Maan and Harbhajan Mann, while they occasionally hum bhajans during chores.
payload.travel_persona: Neelam Bala prefers well‑planned short trips to nearby hill stations like Shimla and Manali or cultural visits to Amritsar’s Golden Temple, balancing adventure with budget constraints and they often travel with a close circle of family friends.
payload.culinary_persona: Neelam Bala’s palate favors authentic regional flavors, regularly preparing sarson da saag, makki di roti, gur‑laden laddoos and herb‑infused tea with mint and coriander, while they enjoy experimenting with seasonal vegetables from their balcony garden.
payload.persona: Neelam Bala blends disciplined household management with a competitive streak, balancing meticulous budgeting and cooking while they occasionally worry‑driven over‑thinking.
payload.cultural_background: Neelam Bala belongs to the Punjabi Hindu community of Jalandhar. Growing up in an urban but tightly‑knit neighbourhood, she has been immersed in the vibrant customs of Punjab – from festive celebrations like Lohri and Diwali to the warmth of joint‑family gatherings. Her cultural upbringing emphasizes respect for elders, a love for Punjabi folk music and dance, and a strong sense of duty towards the household. These influences, combined with the practical, no‑nonsense ethos of city life, shape her preference for orderly routines, dependable habits, and a focus on tangible outcomes.
payload.linguistic_background: Neelam’s mother tongue is Punjabi, spoken in the Gurmukhi script, and she uses it for daily communication with family and neighbours. She is also fluent in Hindi, which she reads and writes using Devanagari, mainly for interacting with the wider community and accessing basic services. Her formal schooling ended before primary level, so her literacy is limited to simple reading and writing in both languages, and she has only a rudimentary grasp of English, sufficient for recognizing common words on product labels.
payload.skills_and_expertise: Neelam is adept at managing a household efficiently, budgeting monthly expenses, and planning meals for her family. She excels in preparing authentic Punjabi dishes such as sarson da saag, makki di roti, and various tiffin items. Her craftsmanship includes traditional Phulkari embroidery and basic sewing, which she occasionally uses for repairing clothing. Despite limited formal schooling, she possesses basic literacy in Punjabi (Gurmukhi) and Hindi (Devanagari) and can handle simple correspondence. She also has rudimentary computer skills, enough to use a basic spreadsheet for tracking household finances.
payload.skills_and_expertise_list: ["Household budgeting", "Punjabi cooking (authentic dishes)", "Traditional Phulkari embroidery", "Basic sewing and mending", "Simple record‑keeping with spreadsheets", "Basic literacy in Punjabi and Hindi", "Organising family events"]
payload.hobbies_and_interests: Neelam enjoys reading Punjabi short stories and poetry, tending to a small balcony garden where she grows herbs and seasonal flowers, experimenting with traditional Punjabi recipes, especially making sweets like gur and laddoo. She practices basic embroidery (Phulkari) as a calming activity and listens to bhajans and kirtans. To manage stress, she does light yoga and meditation. Occasionally, she joins a close‑knit group of friends for chai and gossip, but generally prefers solitary or small‑group activities.
payload.hobbies_and_interests_list: ["Reading Punjabi literature", "Gardening", "Traditional cooking", "Embroidery (Phulkari)", "Listening to devotional music", "Yoga and meditation"]
payload.career_goals_and_ambitions: Neelam's primary career ambition is to transform her household management expertise into a modest home‑based catering service that provides tiffin meals to local office workers. She seeks to complete an adult literacy certification to sharpen her reading, writing, and numeracy, and to acquire basic computer training for efficient bookkeeping. Within the next three to five years, she aims to be recognized for her punctual, high‑quality Punjabi cuisine, expand her client base, and contribute financially to her ageing parents. She also hopes to develop a small network of fellow homemakers for mutual support, reflecting her competitive drive to excel in a niche market.

payload.system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Use ONLY allow-list TYPE names. Billing aliases: EMAIL/CONTACT_EMAIL → EMAIL_ADDRESS; ADDRESS_NUMBER → RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS; RECEIPT_NUMBER and STATE are NOT types (receipt no. / state stay plain prose; geography uses DISTRICT).

- PAN must match ^[A-Z]{5}[0-9]{4}[A-Z]$ e.g. ABCDE1234F — not KAAPR1234C-style junk.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Maternal_health must be age-plausible: routine ANC/delivery for ages ~18–40; ages 40–42 → high-risk ANC / PNC / contraception — never invent extreme late pregnancy (e.g. 49yo as routine 28-week gestation).

- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

  [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]

  [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]]

  [[GENDER|Female]]

  [[INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER|POL-PB-2024-7781]]

  [[TELEPHONE_LANDLINE|0512-2345678]]

  [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|UP78AB1234]]
  [[VILLAGE|Shivpur]]
  [[VOTER_ID|ABC1234567]]
  [[WARD_NUMBER|B2]]

Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content;
  geriatric / extreme-age routine 28-week pregnancy.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; maternal content is
  age-plausible (no unsafe extreme late pregnancy as a routine story).

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs (billing: EMAIL→EMAIL_ADDRESS, ADDRESS→RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS;
  receipt/state/money stay plain prose). Valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
payload.user_prompt:
Write one complete PHC Register Entry (Primary Health Centre) for clinical domain Tuberculosis & Non-Communicable Diseases.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Punjabi (code=pa, script=Gurmukhi). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Tuberculosis & Non-Communicable Diseases — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Punjab, district=Jalandhar.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Gurmukhi for Punjabi.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Female
- age: 29
- marital_status: Never Married
- occupation: No Occupation / Retired / Homemaker
- education_level: Below Primary
- zone: Urban
- state: Punjab
- district: Jalandhar
- first_language: Punjabi
- clinical_persona_summary: A 29-year-old female from Jalandhar, Punjab, never married, with below-primary education and an urban residence. She is a homemaker fluent in Punjabi and Hindi, managing household budgeting and cooking. Her lifestyle includes daily walks and yoga, and she follows Punjabi cultural customs.

Document format guide for PHC Register Entry (Primary Health Centre) (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
PHC register — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|PHC ...]] Village [[VILLAGE|...]] District [[DISTRICT|...]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|...]] [[AGE|19]] [[GENDER|Female]] | brief entry line

MANDATORY inline entities (11 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- VILLAGE — Village e.g. [[VILLAGE|Shivpur]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- ASHA_WORKER_NAME — ASHA Worker Name e.g. [[ASHA_WORKER_NAME|Sunita Yadav]]
- BPL_RATION_CARD — BPL / Ration Card Number e.g. [[BPL_RATION_CARD|BPL-UP-208001-4421]]
- VOTER_ID — Voter ID e.g. [[VOTER_ID|ABC1234567]]
- ABHA_ID — ABHA ID e.g. [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

[[PATIENT_NAME|Gurpreet Kaur]] [[AGE|29]] [[GENDER|Female]] | TB Screening & NCD Checkup

Date of visit: 15 May 2024. Patient presented with a history of productive cough for the past three weeks, associated with low-grade fever in the evenings. She reports no history of contact with known TB patients. As part of the NCD screening protocol, her blood pressure was recorded at 140/90 mmHg and a random blood sugar was checked, which was 110 mg/dL. [[ASHA_WORKER_NAME|Jaswinder Kaur]] was informed for follow-up and contact tracing in the area. Patient's [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]] was noted for communication. Her [[BPL_RATION_CARD|BPL-PB-JLR-2023-5678]] and [[VOTER_ID|PBJ1234567]] details are on file. Her [[ABHA_ID|PB-1234-5678-9012]] has been linked to this encounter. Awaiting GeneXpert results. payload.user_prompt:
Write one complete Prescription for clinical domain Tuberculosis & Non-Communicable Diseases.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Sanskrit (code=sa, script=Devanagari). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Tuberculosis & Non-Communicable Diseases — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Rajasthan, district=Bundi.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Devanagari for Sanskrit.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Female
- age: 19
- marital_status: Never Married
- occupation: No Occupation / Retired / Homemaker
- education_level: Higher Secondary/Intermediate Pre-University/Senior Secondary
- zone: Urban
- state: Rajasthan
- district: Bundi
- first_language: Sanskrit
- clinical_persona_summary: A 19-year-old female from Bundi, Rajasthan, who is a retired homemaker with higher secondary education, lives in an urban setting, and speaks Sanskrit as her primary language. She is never married and follows a routine-driven, solitary lifestyle, often engaging in quiet study and traditional household tasks. Her cultural background includes Rajput heritage and local festivals, and she has a background in classical arts and traditional cooking.

Document format guide for Prescription (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
Prescription — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]]
Patient [[PATIENT_NAME|...]], [[AGE|28]] / [[GENDER|Male]], MRN [[MRN|RX-1001]]
Dr. [[DOCTOR_NAME|...]]
Rx: <drug dose frequency>  (do NOT tag drug names as entities)

MANDATORY inline entities (11 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS — Residential Address e.g. [[RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS|12 Gandhi Nagar, Ward 4]]
- PATIENT_ID — Patient ID e.g. [[PATIENT_ID|PID-77821]]
- ABHA_ID — ABHA ID e.g. [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.
payload.generated_text:
Prescription — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Bundi District Hospital]]
Patient [[PATIENT_NAME|Ananya Sharma]], [[AGE|19]] / [[GENDER|Female]], MRN [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
Dr. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Rajesh Kumar]]
Phone: [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]]
Address: [[RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS|12 Gandhi Nagar, Ward 4, Bundi]]
District: [[DISTRICT|Bundi]], Rajasthan
Patient ID: [[PATIENT_ID|PID-2024-0815-001]]
ABHA ID: [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]

Diagnosis: Pulmonary Tuberculosis with Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus

Rx:
1. Isoniazid 300mg - one tablet daily for 2 months, then 150mg daily for 4 months
2. Rifampicin 450mg - one tablet daily for 6 months
3. Pyrazinamide 1500mg - one tablet daily for 2 months
4. Ethambutol 750mg - one tablet daily for 2 months
5. Metformin 500mg - one tablet twice daily with meals for diabetes management
6. Vitamin B6 25mg - one tablet daily to prevent neuropathy

Follow-up: Review in 2 weeks for sputum examination and blood sugar monitoring. Continue DOTS therapy as per Revised National Tuberculosis Control Programme guidelines. Monitor for drug side effects and maintain diabetes control through diet and regular exercise.

Prescribed by: [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Rajesh Kumar]]
Date: 15 August 2024

payload.professional_persona: Sobha Devi is an aspiring Sanskrit teacher who melds their precise shloka recitation with a practical, routine‑driven approach to household accounts and traditional Rajasthani cooking, though they sometimes let their relaxed attitude lead to a scattered desk.

payload.arts_persona: Sobha Devi immerses themselves in classical bhajans and ragas, admires the vocal mastery of Pandit Jasraj and the sarod brilliance of Ustad Amjad Ali Khan, and they channel their artistic flair into intricate rangoli designs and traditional hand‑embroidery during festivals.
payload.travel_persona: Sobha Devi plans modest trips, preferring short train journeys to Jaipur for university visits and occasional pilgrimages to Varanasi, while they delight in weekend outings to nearby hill‑tops of Mount Abu and the desert dunes of Jaisalmer when savings allow.
payload.culinary_persona: Sobha Devi favors authentic Rajasthani flavors, often preparing dal‑baati‑churma, gatte ki sabzi, and ker‑sangri using bajra, ghee, and besan, while they appreciate the sweet delicacy of Ghevar topped with almond slivers, though they admit that cooking large meals sometimes overwhelms their routine.
payload.persona: Sobha Devi is a routine‑driven, solitary Sanskrit scholar who balances meticulous devotion to tradition with a laid‑back, occasionally disorganized lifestyle, and they relish quiet moments of study.
payload.cultural_background: Sobha Devi grew up in Bundi, a historic town in the heart of Rajasthan, steeped in Rajput heritage and traditional Brahmin customs. Her family follows age‑old rituals, celebrates local festivals like Gangaur and Teej, and places great importance on Sanskrit learning and temple worship. Her daily life follows a well‑established routine of early morning prayers, Sanskrit study, and household chores, reflecting her preference for familiarity and practicality.
payload.linguistic_background: Sobha’s primary language is Sanskrit; she reads, writes, and speaks it fluently, having been taught from an early age by her scholar parents. Through her higher secondary schooling, she has acquired basic literacy in Hindi and can understand simple instructions, but she does not use Hindi for daily conversation. Her exposure to English is limited to textbook terms, and she cannot converse fluently. She prefers the predictable structure of Sanskrit grammar, and her limited engagement with Hindi and English reflects her comfort within familiar linguistic routines.
payload.skills_and_expertise: Sobha’s skill set revolves around her deep knowledge of Sanskrit and practical household management. She can accurately recite and interpret Sanskrit shlokas, prepare authentic Rajasthani dishes such as dal‑baati‑churma, hand‑embroider traditional patterns, keep meticulous household accounts, and guide family members in basic yoga and meditation practices. She approaches these tasks methodically, adhering to time‑tested routines that suit her preference for order and practicality.
payload.skills_and_expertise_list: ['Sanskrit recitation', 'Traditional Rajasthani cooking', 'Hand embroidery', 'Household budgeting', 'Yoga and meditation guidance']
payload.hobbies_and_interests: She finds peace in solitary pursuits: reading classical Sanskrit epics, listening to bhajans and classical ragas, practicing gentle yoga sequences, strolling through the orchards surrounding Bundi, and creating intricate rangoli patterns during festivals. These activities align with her preference for quiet, routine‑based recreation.
payload.hobbies_and_interests_list: ['Reading Sanskrit epics', 'Listening to bhajans', 'Practicing yoga', 'Walking in orchard', 'Creating rangoli designs']
payload.career_goals_and_ambitions: She aspires to enroll in a Bachelor’s program in Sanskrit literature at a university in Jaipur, aiming to become a qualified teacher or researcher. In the long term, Sobha wishes to serve as a priestess in her family’s home temple, preserve temple rituals, and possibly launch a small home‑based venture offering Sanskrit tutoring and handcrafted Rajasthani artefacts. She intends to follow a disciplined academic schedule and integrate her routine‑oriented mindset into her professional growth.
payload.persona_clinical_summary: A 19-year-old female from Bundi, Rajasthan, who is a retired homemaker with higher secondary education, speaks Sanskrit, Hindi, and English, and is never married. She lives in an urban zone and follows traditional Brahmin customs, often engaging in solitary yoga and local community activities. Her lifestyle is routine-driven with occasional disorganization, and she enjoys quiet study and traditional cooking.

Patient reports occasional anxiety related to diagnosis and treatment. Family history negative for malignancies. Lifestyle includes yoga practice and occasional street food consumption. payload.professional_persona: Purani Devi, a 38‑year‑old home‑based bamboo crafts entrepreneur, blends her mastery of traditional ornaments and household budgeting to launch a small venture that trains local women, yet her spontaneous approach and healthy skepticism often lead her to postpone formal paperwork and miss tight deadlines.
payload.linguistic_persona: Purani Devi, a 38‑year‑old native Santali speaker, reads and writes in the Ol Chiki script, handles everyday conversations in Hindi, and occasionally grasps English from radio, though her proficiency remains limited.
payload.sports_persona: Purani Devi, a 38‑year‑old who enjoys casual matches of kabaddi and weekly walks in the paddy fields, occasionally follows the Jharkhand women's Kabaddi team, but prefers solitary fitness through gardening rather than structured gym routines.
payload.arts_persona: Purani Devi, a 38‑year‑old folk song enthusiast, regularly sings regional folk melodies, weaves bamboo ornaments echoing village motifs, sketches the surrounding fields with a fondness for Chhau dance scenes, and draws inspiration from Baul singer Paban Das Baul and Chhau maestro Suryakant Mishra.
payload.travel_persona: Purani Devi, a 38‑year‑old who prefers spontaneous short trips to nearby hill stations like Ranchi and Jamshedpur, enjoys weekend pilgrimages to local temples and the sacred grove, and dreams of exploring the cultural richness of Kolkata and Varanasi when finances allow.
payload.culinary_persona: Purani Devi, a 38‑year‑old who delights in preparing traditional bamboo shoot curry, smoked fish with mustard greens, and simple rice dishes like pakhala, enjoys the tangy flavor of raw mango chutney, but rarely cooks for herself, preferring to share meals with family and neighbors.
payload.persona: Purani Devi, 38, is a curious yet reserved craftsperson who balances family duties with a budding bamboo venture, often leaping into creative projects while hesitating over formal commitments.
payload.cultural_background: Purani belongs to the Santal tribal community of Dumka, Jharkhand, a region where tribal customs intersect with mainstream Hindu festivals. Her family observes traditional Santal rites, such as the Sohrai harvest ceremony, while also participating in Hindu celebrations like Diwali and Navratri. The rural setting, characterized by paddy fields, bamboo groves, and community sangras, shapes a lifestyle that values collective labor, oral storytelling, and reverence for nature.
payload.linguistic_background: Purani's mother tongue is Santali, which she speaks fluently, reads and writes using the Ol Chiki script learned during her primary schooling. She also has functional literacy in Hindi, being able to understand and converse in simple Hindi due to the regional curriculum, though she seldom reads Hindi literature. English proficiency is limited to occasional exposure through radio and television.
payload.skills_and_expertise: Purani excels in household management, preparing traditional Santal meals, budgeting for a large family, and organizing community gatherings. She is skilled in bamboo handicrafts, weaving, and creating folk ornaments. Her knowledge of Santali oral traditions enables her to narrate stories and sing folk songs authentically. She also possesses basic literacy in Ol Chiki and functional Hindi, allowing her to keep simple records and communicate with neighbours.
payload.skills_and_expertise_list: ['Household management', 'Traditional Santal cooking', 'Budgeting', 'Bamboo handicrafts', 'Weaving', 'Folk singing', 'Oral storytelling', 'Basic Ol Chiki literacy', 'Functional Hindi communication']
payload.hobbies_and_interests: Purani enjoys singing traditional Santal folk songs, crafting bamboo ornaments, gardening with local vegetables, and listening to radio programmes that discuss tribal arts. She also likes sketching the rural landscape, attending occasional Chhau dance performances, and reading devotional verses in Hindi.
payload.hobbies_and_interests_list: ['Santal folk singing', 'Bamboo ornament crafting', 'Vegetable gardening', 'Listening to tribal arts radio programmes', 'Sketching rural landscapes', 'Attending Chhau dance performances', 'Reading Hindi devotional verses']
payload.career_goals_and_ambitions: Purani aspires to preserve and revitalize Santal cultural heritage within her village. She hopes to start a small home-based bamboo craft venture that can generate supplemental income for her family while training other women in the community. Additionally, she wishes to become a fluent reader of Hindi literature and eventually assist local schools in teaching Santali folk arts to children.
payload.persona_clinical_summary: Purani Devi is a 38-year-old female from Dumka, Jharkhand, who is currently married and retired. She has a primary education and lives in a rural area, where she speaks Santali as her first language and has functional Hindi literacy. Her lifestyle includes traditional Santal customs and a home-based bamboo crafts venture.
payload.system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Use ONLY allow-list TYPE names. Billing aliases: EMAIL/CONTACT_EMAIL → EMAIL_ADDRESS; ADDRESS_NUMBER → RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS; RECEIPT_NUMBER and STATE are NOT types (receipt no. / state stay plain prose; geography uses DISTRICT).

- PAN must match ^[A-Z]{5}[0-9]{4}[A-Z]$ e.g. ABCDE1234F — not KAAPR1234C-style junk.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Maternal_health must be age-plausible: routine ANC/delivery for ages ~18–40; ages 40–42 → high-risk ANC / PNC / contraception — never invent extreme late pregnancy (e.g. 49yo as routine 28-week gestation).

- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content;
  geriatric / extreme-age routine 28-week pregnancy.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; maternal content is
  age-plausible (no unsafe extreme late pregnancy as a routine story).

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs (billing: EMAIL→EMAIL_ADDRESS, ADDRESS→RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS;
  receipt/state/money stay plain prose). Valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:

  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:

GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs (billing: EMAIL→EMAIL_ADDRESS, ADDRESS→RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS;

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):

  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →

GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).

  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
payload.user_prompt:
Write one complete Automated SMS Notifications for clinical domain General Medicine.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Santali (code=sat, script=Ol Chiki). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match General Medicine — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Jharkhand, district=Dumka.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Ol Chiki for Santali.

LENGTH HARD LIMIT: entire SMS ≤ ~500 characters, but you MUST still include every mandatory entity tag below at least once (compact tags OK).

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Female
- age: 38
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: No Occupation / Retired / Homemaker
- education_level: Primary
- zone: Rural
- state: Jharkhand
- district: Dumka
- first_language: Santali
- clinical_persona_summary: A 38-year-old female from Dumka, Jharkhand, who is married and retired, speaks Santali as her first language and has primary education. She resides in a rural area and may have limited formal employment, potentially engaging in home-based craftwork or informal economic activities. Her lifestyle includes traditional cultural practices and limited exposure to formal healthcare systems.

Document format guide for Automated SMS Notifications (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
CRITICAL: keep the WHOLE SMS under ~400 characters / 2–4 sentences. Not a form dump.
MUST tag [[APPOINTMENT_ID|…]] whenever an appointment is mentioned.
Example:
[[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]: appt [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]] at [[HOSPITAL_NAME|PHC Kanpur]] on 21-May 10:30 with [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Sharma]]. Confirm on [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]. MRN [[MRN|MRN-2024-001]].

MANDATORY inline entities (6 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- APPOINTMENT_ID — Appointment ID e.g. [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

Please bring your [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]] and arrive 15 minutes early. Contact [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]] for queries.

payload.professional_persona: Purani Devi, a 38‑year‑old home‑based bamboo crafts entrepreneur, blends her mastery of traditional ornaments and household budgeting to launch a small venture that trains local women, yet her spontaneous approach and healthy skepticism often lead her to postpone formal paperwork and miss tight deadlines.
payload.linguistic_persona: Purani Devi, a 38‑year‑old native Santali speaker, reads and writes in the Ol Chiki script, handles everyday conversations in Hindi, and occasionally grasps English from radio, though her proficiency remains limited.
payload.sports_persona: Purani Devi, a 38‑year‑old who enjoys casual matches of kabaddi and weekly walks in the paddy fields, occasionally follows the Jharkhand women's Kabaddi team, but prefers solitary fitness through gardening rather than structured gym routines.
payload.arts_persona: Purani Devi, a 38‑year‑old folk song enthusiast, regularly sings regional folk melodies, weaves bamboo ornaments echoing village motifs, sketches the surrounding fields with a fondness for Chhau dance scenes, and draws inspiration from Baul singer Paban Das Baul and Chhau maestro Suryakant Mishra.
payload.travel_persona: Purani Devi, a 38‑year‑old who prefers spontaneous short trips to nearby hill stations like Ranchi and Jamshedpur, enjoys weekend pilgrimages to local temples and the sacred grove, and dreams of exploring the cultural richness of Kolkata and Varanasi when finances allow.
payload.culinary_persona: Purani Devi, a 38‑year‑old who delights in preparing traditional bamboo shoot curry, smoked fish with mustard greens, and simple rice dishes like pakhala, enjoys the tangy flavor of raw mango chutney, but rarely cooks for herself, preferring to share meals with family and neighbors.
payload.persona: Purani Devi, 38, is a curious yet reserved craftsperson who balances family duties with a budding bamboo venture, often leaping into creative projects while hesitating over formal commitments.
payload.cultural_background: Purani belongs to the Santal tribal community of Dumka, Jharkhand, a region where tribal customs intersect with mainstream Hindu festivals. Her family observes traditional Santal rites, such as the Sohrai harvest ceremony, while also participating in Hindu celebrations like Diwali and Navratri. The rural setting, characterized by paddy fields, bamboo groves, and community sangras, shapes a lifestyle that values collective labor, oral storytelling, and reverence for nature.
payload.linguistic_background: Purani's mother tongue is Santali, which she speaks fluently, reads and writes using the Ol Chiki script learned during her primary schooling. She also has functional literacy in Hindi, being able to understand and converse in simple Hindi due to the regional curriculum, though she seldom reads Hindi literature. English proficiency is limited to occasional exposure through radio and television.
payload.skills_and_expertise: Purani excels in household management, preparing traditional Santal meals, budgeting for a large family, and organizing community gatherings. She is skilled in bamboo handicrafts, weaving, and creating folk ornaments. Her knowledge of Santali oral traditions enables her to narrate stories and sing folk songs authentically. She also possesses basic literacy in Ol Chiki and functional Hindi, allowing her to keep simple records and communicate with neighbours.
payload.skills_and_expertise_list: ['Household management', 'Traditional Santal cooking', 'Budgeting', 'Bamboo handicrafts', 'Weaving', 'Folk singing', 'Oral storytelling', 'Basic Ol Chiki literacy', 'Functional Hindi communication']
payload.hobbies_and_interests: Purani enjoys singing traditional Santal folk songs, crafting bamboo ornaments, gardening with local vegetables, and listening to radio programmes that discuss tribal arts. She also likes sketching the rural landscape, attending occasional Chhau dance performances, and reading devotional verses in Hindi.
payload.hobbies_and_interests_list: ['Santal folk singing', 'Bamboo ornament crafting', 'Vegetable gardening', 'Listening to tribal arts radio programmes', 'Sketching rural landscapes', 'Attending Chhau dance performances', 'Reading Hindi devotional verses']
payload.career_goals_and_ambitions: Purani aspires to preserve and revitalize Santal cultural heritage within her village. She hopes to start a small home-based bamboo craft venture that can generate supplemental income for her family while training other women in the community. Additionally, she wishes to become a fluent reader of Hindi literature and eventually assist local schools in teaching Santali folk arts to children.
payload.persona_clinical_summary: Purani Devi, 38, is a married homemaker with primary education residing in rural Jharkhand. She speaks Santali and functional Hindi, and is a retired Santal tribal woman. Clinically, she presents as reserved with family-centered responsibilities.

payload.cultural_background: Bholanath hails from a Santali tribal family settled in the rural district of Bankura, West Bengal. payload.linguistic_background: Bholanath's mother tongue is Santali; he is fluent in speaking, reading and writing it using the Ol Chiki script. His middle-school education gave him basic literacy in Hindi, enabling him to understand simple work instructions and fill out routine paperwork. His command of English is limited to a few logistics-related terms he encounters on the job.
payload.skills_and_expertise: Bholanath's core expertise lies in manual loading and unloading of goods in the rural logistics chain. He skillfully handles heavy pallets, estimates weight, and organizes cargo for efficient transport. His methodical approach ensures strict adherence to safety protocols, proficient operation of hand-carts and simple winches, and basic maintenance of loading equipment. He reliably conducts inventory checks and keeps accurate records, often in Santali using the Ol Chiki script. Additionally, his practical curiosity equips him with basic mechanical problem-solving abilities, often employing 'jugaad' to devise quick fixes.

payload.hobbies_and_interests: Bholanath enjoys listening to traditional Santali folk songs and, on occasional village gatherings, playing the dholak. He is an avid cricket fan, spending evenings catching matches on his mobile phone. His curiosity drives him to tinker with small mechanical devices, creating improvised tools (jugaad) and solving simple puzzles. He also values solitary walks through the fields, which offer him peace and a chance to observe nature.
payload.hobbies_and_interests_list: ["listening to Santali folk songs", "playing the dholak", "watching cricket matches", "tinkering with mechanical devices (jugaad)", "solving simple puzzles", "solitary walks in the fields"]
payload.career_goals_and_ambitions: Bholanath aspires to move from a loader to a supervisory position in the logistics sector within the next five years. He plans to enroll in a vocational diploma in supply-chain management and obtain certifications in equipment handling and safety. In the long term, he dreams of setting up his own small transport and loading service that caters to local farms, providing reliable delivery while creating job opportunities for other youths in his village.
payload.persona_clinical_summary: A methodical 18‑year‑old male loader from Bankura, West Bengal, who is never married, has a middle‑school education and speaks Santali as his first language. payload.system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Use ONLY allow-list TYPE names. Billing aliases: EMAIL/CONTACT_EMAIL → EMAIL_ADDRESS; ADDRESS_NUMBER → RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS; RECEIPT_NUMBER and STATE are NOT types (receipt no. / state stay plain prose; geography uses DISTRICT).

- PAN must match ^[A-Z]{5}[0-9]{4}[A-Z]$ e.g. ABCDE1234F — not KAAPR1234C-style junk.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Maternal_health must be age-plausible: routine ANC/delivery for ages ~18–40; ages 40–42 → high-risk ANC / PNC / contraception — never invent extreme late pregnancy (e.g. 49yo as routine 28-week gestation).

- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):

  [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]

  [[IFSC_CODE|SBIN0001234]]
  [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]
  [[INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER|POL-PB-2024-7781]]

Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content;
  geriatric / extreme-age routine 28-week pregnancy.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; maternal content is
  age-plausible (no unsafe extreme late pregnancy as a routine story).

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs (billing: EMAIL→EMAIL_ADDRESS, ADDRESS→RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS;
  receipt/state/money stay plain prose). Valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.

- PAN must match ^[A-Z]{5}[0-9]{4}[A-Z]$ e.g. ABCDE1234F — not KAAPR1234C-style junk.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Maternal_health must be age-plausible: routine ANC/delivery for ages ~18–40; ages 40–42 → high-risk ANC / PNC / contraception — never invent extreme late pregnancy (e.g. 49yo as routine 28-week gestation).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.

- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.
