Prescription — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Thiruvallur Government Medical College Hospital]]
Patient [[PATIENT_NAME|Lakshmi Narayanan]], [[AGE|40]] / [[GENDER|Female]], MRN [[MRN|MRN-TVL-2024-0042]]
Dr. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. S. Vijayalakshmi]]
Date: Today
CC: Low back pain for 2 weeks.
HPI: Patient is a 40-year-old female newspaper courier from Thiruvallur. She presents with a dull, aching pain in the lower back for the past two weeks. The pain is exacerbated by carrying heavy bundles of newspapers and improves with rest. She reports no radiating pain down the legs, numbness, or weakness. She has a history of hypertension, well-controlled on Amlodipine 5mg daily. She denies any recent trauma.
Examination: On examination, the patient is conscious and oriented. Lumbar spine is tender to palpation over L4-L5 region. No signs of scoliosis. Straight leg raise is negative. No motor, sensory, or reflex deficits in the lower limbs.
Investigations: Lumbar spine X-ray shows mild degenerative changes at L4-L5.
Diagnosis: Mechanical low back pain, likely due to occupational strain.
Plan:
1. Prescribed Paracetamol 500mg tablet, one tablet three times a day after food for pain relief.
2. Prescribed Cyclobenzaprine 5mg tablet, one tablet at bedtime for muscle spasm.
3. Advised adequate rest, hot fomentation to the lower back twice daily.
4. Counselled on maintaining proper posture while carrying loads and requested to avoid heavy lifting.
5. Continue Amlodipine 5mg as prescribed for hypertension.
6. Follow up in one week at the outpatient department.
Patient's Concern: Patient expressed a desire to start a small tea kiosk to reduce physical strain. Advised to discuss with family.
[[PATIENT_ID|PID-TVL-7890]]
[[DISTRICT|Thiruvallur]]
[[RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS|15, Gandhi Street, Thiruvallur Town]]
[[PHONE_NUMBER|9840512345]]

payload.system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Use ONLY allow-list TYPE names. Billing aliases: EMAIL/CONTACT_EMAIL → EMAIL_ADDRESS; ADDRESS_NUMBER → RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS; RECEIPT_NUMBER and STATE are NOT types (receipt no. / state stay plain prose; geography uses DISTRICT).

- PAN must match ^[A-Z]{5}[0-9]{4}[A-Z]$ e.g. ABCDE1234F — not KAAPR1234C-style junk.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Maternal_health must be age-plausible: routine ANC/delivery for ages ~18–40; ages 40–42 → high-risk ANC / PNC / contraception — never invent extreme late pregnancy (e.g. 49yo as routine 28-week gestation).

- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Format examples (copy the bracket pattern; invent new synthetic values):

Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content;
  geriatric / extreme-age routine 28-week pregnancy.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; maternal content is
  age-plausible (no unsafe extreme late pregnancy as a routine story).

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs (billing: EMAIL→EMAIL_ADDRESS, ADDRESS→RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS;
  receipt/state/money stay plain prose). Valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.

GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Tamil Nadu, district=Thiruvallur.

In the longer term, he envisions opening his own modest workshop in Chennai that offers affordable maintenance services to small transport operators and local dairy farms, while mentoring apprentices from his community. He also wishes to integrate his interest in art by designing functional, aesthetically pleasing tool storage solutions for his workshop.
payload.persona_clinical_summary: Mr. Mahavir Jain, a 34-year-old male mechanic in urban Chennai, is married with middle school education and speaks Tamil. He works in dairy maintenance, practicing cost-effective improvisation and fault diagnosis. His lifestyle includes daily walks, occasional badminton, and a diet of simple vegetarian fare.
payload.system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Use ONLY allow-list TYPE names. Billing aliases: EMAIL/CONTACT_EMAIL → EMAIL_ADDRESS; ADDRESS_NUMBER → RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS; RECEIPT_NUMBER and STATE are NOT types (receipt no. / state stay plain prose; geography uses DISTRICT).

- PAN must match ^[A-Z]{5}[0-9]{4}[A-Z]$ e.g. ABCDE1234F — not KAAPR1234C-style junk.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Maternal_health must be age-plausible: routine ANC/delivery for ages ~18–40; ages 40–42 → high-risk ANC / PNC / contraception — never invent extreme late pregnancy (e.g. 49yo as routine 28-week gestation).

- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Format examples (copy the bracket pattern; invent new synthetic values):
  [[AADHAAR_NUMBER|157815659387]]
  [[ABHA_ADDRESS|meena.devi@abdm]]
  [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]
  [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-0912]]
  [[AGE|34]]
  [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]]
  [[ASHA_WORKER_NAME|Sunita Yadav]]
  [[BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER|50200012345678]]
  [[BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER|SBIN0001234]]
  [[BED_NUMBER|12]]
  [[BPL_RATION_CARD|BPL-UP-208001-4421]]
  [[CASTE|OBC]]
  [[CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER|4111111111111111]]
  [[CVV|123]]
  [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
  [[DOB|1992-03-14]]
  [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
  [[DRIVING_LICENCE|UP14 20110012345]]
  [[EMAIL_ADDRESS|meena.devi@example.com]]
  [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-77821]]
  [[ENCOUNTER_ID|ENC-55601]]
  [[GENDER|Female]]
  [[HOSPITAL_ID|HSP-UP-014]]
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
  [[IFSC_CODE|SBIN0001234]]
  [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]
  [[INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER|POL-PB-2024-7781]]
  [[IP_ADDRESS|103.21.244.12]]
  [[MAC_ADDRESS|00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E]]
  [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
  [[OCCUPATION|Teacher]]
  [[PAN_NUMBER|ABCDE1234F]]
  [[PASSPORT_NUMBER|Z1234567]]
  [[PATIENT_ID|PID-77821]]
  [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
  [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
  [[PIN|4829]]
  [[PIN_CODE|208001]]
  [[REFERRAL_ID|REF-2024-1123]]
  [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
  [[RELIGION|Hindu]]
  [[RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS|12 Gandhi Nagar, Ward 4]]
  [[STUDENT_ID|STU-2024-0912]]
  [[TAX_ID|29ABCDE1234F1Z5]]
  [[TELEPHONE_LANDLINE|0512-2345678]]
  [[URL|https://example.org/u/meena]]
  [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|UP78AB1234]]
  [[VILLAGE|Shivpur]]
  [[VOTER_ID|ABC1234567]]
  [[WARD_NUMBER|B2]]

Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content;
  geriatric / extreme-age routine 28-week pregnancy.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; maternal content is
  age-plausible (no unsafe extreme late pregnancy as a routine story).

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs (billing: EMAIL→EMAIL_ADDRESS, ADDRESS→RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS;
  receipt/state/money stay plain prose). Valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
payload.user_prompt:
Write one complete ASHA Worker Note for clinical domain Psychiatry / Behavioral Health.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Tamil (code=ta, script=Tamil). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Psychiatry / Behavioral Health — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Tamil Nadu, district=Chennai.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Tamil for Tamil.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Male
- age: 34
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: Mechanic, Dairy Maintenance
- education_level: Middle
- zone: Urban
- state: Tamil Nadu
- district: Chennai
- first_language: Tamil
- clinical_persona_summary: Mr. Mahavir Jain, a 34-year-old male mechanic in urban Chennai, is married with middle school education and speaks Tamil. He works in dairy maintenance, practicing cost-effective improvisation and fault diagnosis. His lifestyle includes daily walks, occasional badminton, and a diet of simple vegetarian fare.

Document format guide for ASHA Worker Note (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
ASHA note — Village [[VILLAGE|...]], District [[DISTRICT|...]]
Beneficiary [[PATIENT_NAME|...]], [[AGE|26]] / [[GENDER|Female]]
ASHA: [[ASHA_WORKER_NAME|...]] | Phone [[PHONE_NUMBER|98XXXXXXXX]]
Visit findings: <2-4 short sentences>

MANDATORY inline entities (11 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- ASHA_WORKER_NAME — ASHA Worker Name e.g. [[ASHA_WORKER_NAME|Sunita Yadav]]
- VILLAGE — Village e.g. [[VILLAGE|Shivpur]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- RELATIVE_NAME — Relative Name (Father/Mother/Spouse/Guardian) e.g. [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
- BPL_RATION_CARD — BPL / Ration Card Number e.g. [[BPL_RATION_CARD|BPL-UP-208001-4421]]
- VOTER_ID — Voter ID e.g. [[VOTER_ID|ABC1234567]]
- RELIGION — Religion e.g. [[RELIGION|Hindu]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.
payload.generated_text:
ASHA note — Village [[VILLAGE|T. Nagar]], District [[DISTRICT|Chennai]]
Beneficiary [[PATIENT_NAME|Mahavir Jain]], [[AGE|34]] / [[GENDER|Male]]
ASHA: [[ASHA_WORKER_NAME|Lakshmi Devi]] | Phone [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]]
Visit findings: Patient reports increased irritability and sleep disturbances over past 3 weeks. Expresses work-related stress and financial concerns affecting daily functioning.

Chief complaint: Patient states feeling "overwhelmed" with work pressure and family responsibilities. Reports difficulty concentrating at work and occasional headaches.

Assessment: Patient appears anxious with psychomotor agitation. Mood appears depressed with reduced affect. No suicidal ideation reported. Sleep pattern disrupted with early morning awakening.

Plan: Referred to District Hospital Psychiatry OPD for evaluation. Prescribed stress management techniques and advised regular exercise. Follow-up scheduled in 1 week.

Family history: Mother has hypertension. Father is diabetic. Brother is healthy.

Social history: Married with two children. Lives in joint family setup. Works as dairy maintenance mechanic.

[[RELATIVE_NAME|Sita Jain]] (Wife) present during counseling session. [[BPL_RATION_CARD|BPL-TN-400017-8923]] verified. [[VOTER_ID|TN2345678]] confirmed. [[RELIGION|Hindu]]

payload.professional_persona: Mahavir Jain, a seasoned mechanic with over ten years of hands‑on experience across motor vehicles, agricultural gear and dairy processing equipment, excels at fault diagnosis, cost‑effective improvisation (jugaad), and they are now eyeing senior technician certification in advanced diesel diagnostics while dreaming of a modest workshop that blends functional design with aesthetic tool storage.
payload.linguistic_persona: Mahavir Jain, a native Tamil speaker who reads and writes fluently in Tamil, uses functional English for work manuals and everyday communication, and they have limited exposure to Hindi, mainly through occasional public signage.
payload.sports_persona: Mahavir Jain follows the Chennai Super Kings fervently, plays weekend badminton at a nearby community center, and they maintain their fitness with daily morning walks along Marina Beach and occasional cricket batting practice.
payload.arts_persona: Mahavir Jain enjoys listening to the soulful renditions of M.S. Subbulakshmi and the compositions of Ilaiyaraaja, watches classic Tamil films starring Rajinikanth and Kamal Haasan, and spends evenings sketching minimalist line drawings of temples and machinery, finding inspiration in the aesthetic harmony of both.
payload.travel_persona: Mahavir Jain prefers budget-friendly road and train trips, has explored Pondicherry’s French quarter, trekked the misty hills of Kodaikanal, and they dream of witnessing the sunrise at Rishikesh, balancing their love for solitude with occasional family outings.
payload.culinary_persona: Mahavir Jain favors simple South‑Indian vegetarian fare such as idli, dosa with coconut chutney, sambar, rasam, and curd rice, relishes the seasonal mangoes and enjoys occasional sweet treats like Mysore Pak, but rarely cooks at home, preferring freshly prepared meals from trusted neighborhood stalls.
payload.persona: Mahavir Jain is a methodical, compassionate mechanic who merges his love for art and non‑violence with practical ingenuity, saving diligently while dreaming of a modest, aesthetically‑inspired workshop.
payload.cultural_background: Mahavir grew up in a traditional Jain family in the bustling urban neighbourhood of Mylapore, Chennai. His upbringing was steeped in Tamil customs—celebrations of Pongal, listening to Carnatic music, and a love for simple home‑cooked vegetarian meals. At the same time, Jain principles of ahimsa and strict vegetarianism guided his daily life, and he regularly visits the local Jain temple for Mahavir Jayanti and Paryushana. The blend of Tamil urban culture and Jain spiritual discipline shaped his worldview, giving him a deep respect for harmony, modesty, and community service.
payload.linguistic_background: Mahavir's mother tongue is Tamil, which he reads, writes, and speaks fluently. He completed middle school, giving him functional literacy in Tamil and basic reading and writing skills in English used for work manuals and simple communication. He has no formal training in Hindi, so his Hindi exposure is minimal, limited to occasional signage in Chennai. Overall, his linguistic repertoire is dominated by Tamil, supplemented by functional English for occupational purposes.
payload.skills_and_expertise: Mahavir is a hands‑on mechanic with over a decade of experience in repairing and maintaining a variety of motor vehicles, agricultural machinery, and dairy processing equipment. He excels at diagnosing faults, performing preventive maintenance, and improvising cost‑effective solutions (jugaad) when spare parts are scarce. His work in dairy maintenance includes servicing milk chilling units, pasteurizers, and automated milking systems, ensuring hygiene standards are met. Additionally, he possesses good manual dexterity, basic electrical wiring skills, and a cooperative attitude that makes him reliable for team‑based troubleshooting.
payload.skills_and_expertise_list: ["Mechanical repair", "Agricultural machinery maintenance", "Dairy equipment servicing", "Fault diagnosis", "Preventive maintenance", "Jugaad improvisation", "Electrical wiring basics", "Team troubleshooting", "Customer service"]
payload.hobbies_and_interests: In his spare time, Mahavir enjoys sketching simple line drawings of machines and local temples, listening to Carnatic music, and watching classic Tamil movies. He is a regular visitor to the local library where he reads Jain philosophical texts and Tamil poetry. His curiosity drives him to experiment with small DIY projects, such as building a portable solar charger for his tools. He also likes taking solitary evening walks along Marina Beach, where he finds quiet reflection.
payload.hobbies_and_interests_list: ["Sketching machines", "Listening to Carnatic music", "Watching classic Tamil movies", "Reading Jain philosophy", "DIY electronics projects", "Evening walks on Marina Beach", "Reading Tamil poetry"]
payload.career_goals_and_ambitions: Mahavir aspires to become a senior technician specialising in advanced diesel engine diagnostics and sustainable dairy technology. He plans to enroll in a certification program for diesel mechanics and attend workshops on energy‑efficient refrigeration for dairy units. In the longer term, he envisions opening his own modest workshop in Chennai that offers affordable maintenance services to small transport operators and local dairy farms, while mentoring apprentices from his community. He also wishes to integrate his interest in art by designing functional, aesthetically pleasing tool storage solutions for his workshop.
payload.persona_clinical_summary: Mr. Mahavir Jain, a 34-year-old male mechanic in Chennai, holds middle education and is married. He speaks Tamil fluently and works in an urban setting, primarily maintaining dairy equipment. His lifestyle includes daily walks along Marina Beach and occasional badminton, with a preference for simple vegetarian meals.

payload.user_prompt:
Write one complete Automated SMS Notifications for clinical domain General Medicine.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Tamil (code=ta, script=Tamil). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match General Medicine — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Tamil Nadu, district=Chennai.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Tamil for Tamil.

LENGTH HARD LIMIT: entire SMS ≤ ~500 characters, but you MUST still include every mandatory entity tag below at least once (compact tags OK).

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Male
- age: 34
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: Mechanic, Dairy Maintenance
- education_level: Middle
- zone: Urban
- state: Tamil Nadu
- district: Chennai
- first_language: Tamil
- clinical_persona_summary: Mr. Mahavir Jain, a 34-year-old male mechanic in Chennai, holds middle education and is married. He speaks Tamil fluently and works in an urban setting, primarily maintaining dairy equipment. His lifestyle includes daily walks along Marina Beach and occasional badminton, with a preference for simple vegetarian meals.

Document format guide for Automated SMS Notifications (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
CRITICAL: keep the WHOLE SMS under ~400 characters / 2–4 sentences. Not a form dump.
MUST tag [[APPOINTMENT_ID|…]] whenever an appointment is mentioned.
Example:
[[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]: appt [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]] at [[HOSPITAL_NAME|PHC Kanpur]] on 21-May 10:30 with [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Sharma]]. Confirm on [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]. MRN [[MRN|MRN-2024-001]].

MANDATORY inline entities (6 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- APPOINTMENT_ID — Appointment ID e.g. [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

payload.arts_persona: Mahavir Jain enjoys listening to the soulful renditions of M.S. Subbulakshmi and the compositions of Ilaiyaraaja, watches classic Tamil films starring Rajinikanth and Kamal Haasan, and spends evenings sketching minimalist line drawings of temples and machinery, finding inspiration in the aesthetic harmony of both.
payload.travel_persona: Mahavir Jain prefers budget-friendly road and train trips, has explored Pondicherry’s French quarter, trekked the misty hills of Kodaikanal, and they dream of witnessing the sunrise at Rishikesh, balancing their love for solitude with occasional family outings.
payload.culinary_persona: Mahavir Jain favors simple South‑Indian vegetarian fare such as idli, dosa with coconut chutney, sambar, rasam, and curd rice, relishes the seasonal mangoes and enjoys occasional sweet treats like Mysore Pak, but rarely cooks at home, preferring freshly prepared meals from trusted neighborhood stalls.
payload.persona: Mahavir Jain is a methodical, compassionate mechanic who merges his love for art and non‑violence with practical ingenuity, saving diligently while dreaming of a modest, aesthetically‑inspired workshop.
payload.cultural_background: Mahavir grew up in a traditional Jain family in the bustling urban neighbourhood of Mylapore, Chennai. His upbringing was steeped in Tamil customs—celebrations of Pongal, listening to Carnatic music, and a love for simple home‑cooked vegetarian meals. At the same time, Jain principles of ahimsa and strict vegetarianism guided his daily life, and he regularly visits the local Jain temple for Mahavir Jayanti and Paryushana. The blend of Tamil urban culture and Jain spiritual discipline shaped his worldview, giving him a deep respect for harmony, modesty, and community service.
payload.linguistic_background: Mahavir's mother tongue is Tamil, which he reads, writes, and speaks fluently. He completed middle school, giving him functional literacy in Tamil and basic reading and writing skills in English used for work manuals and simple communication. He has no formal training in Hindi, so his Hindi exposure is minimal, limited to occasional signage in Chennai. Overall, his linguistic repertoire is dominated by Tamil, supplemented by functional English for occupational purposes.
payload.skills_and_expertise: Mahavir is a hands‑on mechanic with over a decade of experience in repairing and maintaining a variety of motor vehicles, agricultural machinery, and dairy processing equipment. He excels at diagnosing faults, performing preventive maintenance, and improvising cost‑effective solutions (jugaad) when spare parts are scarce. His work in dairy maintenance includes servicing milk chilling units, pasteurizers, and automated milking systems, ensuring hygiene standards are met. Additionally, he possesses good manual dexterity, basic electrical wiring skills, and a cooperative attitude that makes him reliable for team‑based troubleshooting.
payload.skills_and_expertise_list: ["Mechanical repair", "Agricultural machinery maintenance", "Dairy equipment servicing", "Fault diagnosis", "Preventive maintenance", "Jugaad improvisation", "Electrical wiring basics", "Team troubleshooting", "Customer service"]
payload.hobbies_and_interests: In his spare time, Mahavir enjoys sketching simple line drawings of machines and local temples, listening to Carnatic music, and watching classic Tamil movies. He is a regular visitor to the local library where he reads Jain philosophical texts and Tamil poetry. His curiosity drives him to experiment with small DIY projects, such as building a portable solar charger for his tools. He also likes taking solitary evening walks along Marina Beach, where he finds quiet reflection.
payload.hobbies_and_interests_list: ["Sketching machines", "Listening to Carnatic music", "Watching classic Tamil movies", "Reading Jain philosophy", "DIY electronics projects", "Evening walks on Marina Beach", "Reading Tamil poetry"]
payload.career_goals_and_ambitions: Mahavir aspires to become a senior technician specialising in advanced diesel engine diagnostics and sustainable dairy technology. He plans to enroll in a certification program for diesel mechanics and attend workshops on energy‑efficient refrigeration for dairy units. In the longer term, he envisions opening his own modest workshop in Chennai that offers affordable maintenance services to small transport operators and local dairy farms, while mentoring apprentices from his community. He also wishes to integrate his interest in art by designing functional, aesthetically pleasing tool storage solutions for his workshop.
payload.persona_clinical_summary: Mr. Mahavir Jain, 34, is a married mechanic in Chennai, Tamil Nadu, with middle education and Tamil as his first language. He works in dairy maintenance and is currently employed in an urban setting. His lifestyle includes daily walks along Marina Beach and a preference for simple South-Indian vegetarian meals.

Patient History:
Mr. Mahavir Jain, a 34-year-old male mechanic from Chennai, presented with persistent cough for 3 weeks, low-grade fever, and weight loss of 5 kg. payload.system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Use ONLY allow-list TYPE names. Billing aliases: EMAIL/CONTACT_EMAIL → EMAIL_ADDRESS; ADDRESS_NUMBER → RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS; RECEIPT_NUMBER and STATE are NOT types (receipt no. / state stay plain prose; geography uses DISTRICT).

- PAN must match ^[A-Z]{5}[0-9]{4}[A-Z]$ e.g. ABCDE1234F — not KAAPR1234C-style junk.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Maternal_health must be age-plausible: routine ANC/delivery for ages ~18–40; ages 40–42 → high-risk ANC / PNC / contraception — never invent extreme late pregnancy (e.g. 49yo as routine 28-week gestation).

- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content;
  geriatric / extreme-age routine 28-week pregnancy.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; maternal content is
  age-plausible (no unsafe extreme late pregnancy as a routine story).

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs (billing: EMAIL→EMAIL_ADDRESS, ADDRESS→RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS;
  receipt/state/money stay plain prose). Valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.

GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Maharashtra, district=Nanded.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION — Vehicle Registration Number e.g. [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|UP78AB1234]]
- EMAIL_ADDRESS — Email Address e.g. [[EMAIL_ADDRESS|meena.devi@example.com]]

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.

Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content;
  geriatric / extreme-age routine 28-week pregnancy.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; maternal content is
  age-plausible (no unsafe extreme late pregnancy as a routine story).

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs (billing: EMAIL→EMAIL_ADDRESS, ADDRESS→RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS;
  receipt/state/money stay plain prose). Valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
payload.user_prompt:
Write one complete Discharge Summary for clinical domain General Medicine.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Telugu (code=te, script=Telugu). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match General Medicine — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Maharashtra, district=Nanded.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Telugu for Telugu.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Male
- age: 18
- marital_status: Never Married
- occupation: No Occupation / Retired / Homemaker
- education_level: Primary
- zone: Urban
- state: Maharashtra
- district: Nanded
- first_language: Telugu
- clinical_persona_summary: An 18‑year‑old male, never married, with primary education, living in an urban area of Nanded, Maharashtra, who speaks Telugu as his first language and Marathi functionally. He is retired or a homemaker, with a relaxed lifestyle that includes sketching and occasional street cricket. His background is from a Telugu family in a multilingual Maharashtrian setting.

Document format guide for Discharge Summary (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
Discharge Summary — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|...]] DOB [[DOB|1994-06-21]] [[AGE|30]] [[GENDER|Female]]
Adm [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|...]] Ward [[WARD_NUMBER|B2]] Bed [[BED_NUMBER|12]]
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|...]] | Course / advice: <concise paragraphs matching domain + persona sex/age>

MANDATORY inline entities (14 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- DOB — Date of Birth e.g. [[DOB|1992-03-14]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- ADMISSION_NUMBER — Admission Number e.g. [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-0912]]
- WARD_NUMBER — Ward Number e.g. [[WARD_NUMBER|B2]]
- BED_NUMBER — Bed Number e.g. [[BED_NUMBER|12]]
- RELATIVE_NAME — Relative Name (Father/Mother/Spouse/Guardian) e.g. [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- ABHA_ADDRESS — ABHA Address e.g. [[ABHA_ADDRESS|meena.devi@abdm]]
- CASTE — Caste e.g. [[CASTE|OBC]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.
payload.generated_text:
Discharge Summary — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Nanded]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|Ravi Kumar]] DOB [[DOB|2006-03-15]] [[AGE|18]] [[GENDER|Male]]
Adm [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-0412]] Ward [[WARD_NUMBER|A1]] Bed [[BED_NUMBER|05]]
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Priya Deshmukh]] | Course / advice: Patient presented with acute gastroenteritis symptoms including vomiting and diarrhea for 2 days. Initial evaluation revealed mild dehydration and electrolyte imbalance. Patient was started on IV fluids and anti-emetic medications. Symptoms improved significantly over 48 hours of hospitalization. Patient tolerating oral intake well. Discharged with oral rehydration solution and dietary instructions. Advised to avoid street food and maintain proper hand hygiene. Follow-up in 3 days at outpatient department.

[[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Kumar]] [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]] [[ABHA_ADDRESS|ravi.kumar.abdm@abdm]] [[CASTE|OBC]] [[DISTRICT|Nanded]]

payload.professional_persona: An aspiring illustrator, Lakshman Bapu, an 18‑year‑old, they blend traditional Telugu and Marathi folk motifs with modern digital art techniques, using their sketching talent and basic computer skills to create culturally rich visual stories.
payload.linguistic_persona: A multilingual teen, Lakshman Bapu, they speak Telugu fluently, understand Marathi conversationally, read basic Hindi and use elementary English mainly for informal chats.
payload.sports_persona: A street cricket enthusiast, Lakshman Bapu, they join the local Nanded Cricket Club on weekends, cheer for Mumbai Indians in the IPL, and practice quick batting drills at the neighborhood ground, though sometimes they skip practice due to a laid‑back attitude.
payload.arts_persona: An emerging visual storyteller, Lakshman Bapu, they admire the mythological paintings of Raja Ravi Varma, the contemporary digital works of Satish Kathuria, and listen to classic Telugu playback singer S. P. Balasubrahmanyam alongside Marathi Lavani star Shakuntala, often sketching urban scenes inspired by Nanded’s street art while experimenting with digital brushes on a smartphone.
payload.travel_persona: A curious weekend traveler, Lakshman Bapu, they love quick trips to nearby Aurangabad to explore the Ajanta caves, annual family holidays on Goa’s beaches, and dream of touring Hyderabad’s Charminar and the historic ruins of Hampi, often planning low‑budget journeys using shared bus rides and hostel stays.
payload.culinary_persona: A selective palate, Lakshman Bapu, they favor spicy Telugu fare like Pesarattu with ginger chutney, sweet Marathi bhakri with varan, tangy tamarind rice (pulihora), and often snack on vada pav or samosa during study breaks, though they seldom cook complex meals, sticking mainly to simple family recipes.
payload.persona: An 18‑year‑old visual dreamer, Lakshman Bapu, they sketch mythic tales, fret over worries, juggle school and art with a relaxed, often disorganized schedule, and unwind with street cricket.
payload.cultural_background: Lakshman belongs to a Telugu family that migrated to Nanded in Maharashtra. Growing up in an urban setting, he is immersed in a blend of Telugu, Marathi, and broader Maharashtrian cultures. He celebrates Telugu festivals like Ugadi and Sankranti, as well as Marathi traditions such as Ganesh Chaturthi and Diwali. The city's historic temples and vibrant street life expose him to a mix of Hindu rituals, local folk arts, and contemporary urban influences, fostering his curiosity about diverse cultural expressions.
payload.linguistic_background: Lakshman's linguistic repertoire includes native fluency in Telugu, functional proficiency in Marathi, and basic competence in Hindi. Growing up in a multilingual household and attending a primary school that taught the three regional languages, he can read and write simple sentences in Telugu script, understand spoken Marathi, and read basic Hindi texts. His exposure to English is limited to school basics, and he uses it mainly for informal communication.
payload.skills_and_expertise: Lakshman demonstrates practical multilingual communication skills in Telugu, Marathi and Hindi, coupled with a natural talent for sketching and visual storytelling. He can sing traditional folk songs, create simple digital drawings on a smartphone, and is comfortable handling basic household chores like cooking regional dishes. His primary school education gave him rudimentary computer literacy, allowing him to browse the internet for art inspiration and to use messaging apps across languages.
payload.skills_and_expertise_list: ["Multilingual communication (Telugu, Marathi, Hindi)", "Basic sketching and visual storytelling", "Singing folk songs", "Simple digital drawing on smartphone", "Basic cooking of regional dishes", "Rudimentary computer literacy (internet browsing, messaging apps)"]
payload.hobbies_and_interests: Lakshman enjoys sketching urban scenes and mythological characters, listening to a mix of Telugu film songs, Marathi lavani, and indie playlists on his phone. He watches movies across languages, reads comic books, and experiments with simple cooking recipes from his family's cuisine. On weekends, he plays street cricket with friends, explores local street art, and occasionally captures photos with his smartphone. To manage his worry, he practices brief breathing exercises and writes short journal entries reflecting on new ideas.
payload.hobbies_and_interests_list: ["Sketching urban scenes and mythological characters", "Listening to a mix of Telugu film songs, Marathi lavani, and indie music", "Watching movies across languages", "Reading comic books", "Experimenting with simple regional cooking", "Playing street cricket", "Exploring local street art", "Smartphone photography", "Brief breathing exercises for stress", "Journaling ideas"]
payload.career_goals_and_ambitions: Lakshman aspires to continue his education beyond primary school, ideally enrolling in a vocational diploma in graphic design or fine arts. He dreams of building a portfolio that blends traditional Telugu and Marathi folk motifs with modern digital illustration, aiming to work as a freelance illustrator or join a creative studio. In the long term, he hopes to use his art to promote regional cultural heritage and perhaps teach art workshops for under‑privileged youth in Nanded.
payload.persona_clinical_summary: An 18‑year‑old male from Nanded, Maharashtra, who is unmarried, retired, and has a primary education, presenting with a multilingual background (Telugu, Marathi, Hindi) and an urban residence. He is a visual storyteller with a relaxed, disorganized schedule, engaging in street cricket and expressing anxiety over worries. His lifestyle includes a diet of spicy Telugu and Marathi foods, with occasional snacking on vada pav or samosa.

payload.user_prompt:
Write one complete Referral Letter for clinical domain Surgical.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Telugu (code=te, script=Telugu). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Surgical — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Maharashtra, district=Nanded.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Telugu for Telugu.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Male
- age: 18
- marital_status: Never Married
- occupation: No Occupation / Retired / Homemaker
- education_level: Primary
- zone: Urban
- state: Maharashtra
- district: Nanded
- first_language: Telugu
- clinical_persona_summary: An 18‑year‑old male from Nanded, Maharashtra, who is unmarried, retired, and has a primary education, presenting with a multilingual background (Telugu, Marathi, Hindi) and an urban residence. He is a visual storyteller with a relaxed, disorganized schedule, engaging in street cricket and expressing anxiety over worries. His lifestyle includes a diet of spicy Telugu and Marathi foods, with occasional snacking on vada pav or samosa.

Document format guide for Referral Letter (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
Referral [[REFERRAL_ID|REF-2024-01]] from [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]] / [[DOCTOR_NAME|...]]
Re: [[PATIENT_NAME|...]], [[AGE|36]] / [[GENDER|Male]], District [[DISTRICT|...]]
Reason: <domain-appropriate clinical reason matching persona>

MANDATORY inline entities (12 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- REFERRAL_ID — Referral ID e.g. [[REFERRAL_ID|REF-2024-1123]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- RELATIVE_NAME — Relative Name (Father/Mother/Spouse/Guardian) e.g. [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
- PASSPORT_NUMBER — Passport Number e.g. [[PASSPORT_NUMBER|Z1234567]]
- DRIVING_LICENCE — Driving Licence Number e.g. [[DRIVING_LICENCE|UP14 20110012345]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.
payload.generated_text:
Referral [[REFERRAL_ID|REF-2024-0915]] from [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Government Medical College Hospital Nanded]] / [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Rajesh Kumar]]
Re: [[PATIENT_NAME|Ravi Kumar]], [[AGE|18]] / [[GENDER|Male]], District [[DISTRICT|Nanded]]
Reason: Acute appendicitis requiring surgical intervention

Patient Ravi Kumar, an 18-year-old male from Nanded, presents with acute abdominal pain localized to the right lower quadrant for the past 24 hours. The pain started as dull discomfort after playing street cricket and progressively worsened. On examination, there is tenderness at McBurney's point with rebound tenderness and guarding. Blood tests show elevated white blood cell count at 14,500/mm³. Ultrasound confirms appendiceal diameter of 8mm with surrounding inflammation.

The patient has no significant past medical history. He is unmarried and currently not employed. His father, [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Kumar]], is available for further information and consent. The patient's medical record number is [[MRN|MRN-2024-0915-001]]. Contact can be made at [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]].

For surgical evaluation and management, the patient is referred to the surgical department. His passport number is [[PASSPORT_NUMBER|P123456789]] and driving license number is [[DRIVING_LICENCE|MH042024001234]].

Please advise on the next steps for surgical intervention.

payload.culinary_persona: Bibhuti Raut enjoys hearty meals of rice, fish curry seasoned with mustard oil and tamarind, savors Odia staples like pakhala and rasgulla, relishes Telugu biryani with a side of raita, and often indulges in street‑side puchka, though they rarely cook at home.
payload.persona: Bibhuti Raut is a diligent window cleaner whose artistic curiosity fuels a love for music, sketching and cricket, while their organized mindset and modest frugality keep them grounded, and they stay humble in every task.
payload.cultural_background: Bibhuti Raut hails from a Telugu‑speaking family that settled in the coastal district of Ganjam, Odisha. Growing up in an urban environment, he blends the cultural practices of his Telugu roots—celebrating festivals like Ugadi and Sankranti, enjoying Carnatic music and traditional dance—with the local Odia customs he encounters daily. As a member of the Raut community, he identifies with modest, working‑class values and a strong sense of communal solidarity.
payload.linguistic_background: His mother tongue is Telugu, which he can speak, read, and write at a primary‑school level. Living in Ganjam, he has picked up functional Odia for everyday communication, though he is not formally literate in it. He also understands spoken Hindi from television and local interactions, but his reading and writing skills in Hindi are limited.
payload.skills_and_expertise: His primary expertise lies in window cleaning, where he combines physical stamina with meticulous attention to detail. He is adept at using ladders and safety gear, follows high‑rise safety protocols, and performs routine maintenance of cleaning equipment. Organized and methodical, he plans his daily routes efficiently, manages time well, and ensures consistent service quality. Bibhuti's curiosity about art sharpens his observational skills, enabling him to notice even subtle dust patterns and surface imperfections. He also possesses good interpersonal skills for brief customer interactions, and basic troubleshooting abilities for minor tool repairs.
payload.skills_and_expertise_list: ["Window cleaning", "High‑rise safety protocol compliance", "Use of ladders and safety gear", "Routine equipment maintenance", "Time management", "Route planning", "Attention to detail", "Observational skills", "Customer interaction", "Basic tool troubleshooting"]
payload.hobbies_and_interests: Bibhuti enjoys listening to Telugu film songs and Odia folk music, which fuel his love for rhythm and melody. He often watches regional movies and occasionally sketches simple drawings inspired by the cityscape and temple architecture he encounters during his work. Playing cricket with friends in local grounds, taking evening walks to observe street art, and reading short stories in Telugu are his favorite ways to unwind. He also has a keen interest in learning basic painting techniques through community art workshops whenever time permits.
payload.hobbies_and_interests_list: ["Listening to Telugu and Odia music", "Watching regional movies", "Sketching cityscapes and temple architecture", "Playing cricket with friends", "Evening walks observing street art", "Reading Telugu short stories", "Participating in community art workshops"]
payload.career_goals_and_ambitions: Bibhuti aims to move beyond his current role as a window cleaner by acquiring formal certification in occupational safety and building maintenance. Within the next few years, he aspires to become a supervisor of a cleaning crew, allowing him to oversee larger projects and mentor junior workers. In the longer term, he dreams of starting his own small cleaning service business, which would provide a steadier income for his family and enable him to fund further studies in a vocational trade such as carpentry or plumbing. He also hopes to deepen his appreciation for art by attending community workshops whenever possible.

payload.travel_persona: Bibhuti Raut prefers short weekend trips to nearby coastal spots like Puri and Konark, plans travel via affordable train tickets, enjoys simple guesthouse stays, dreams of visiting Tirupati for a pilgrimage and hopes to someday explore Bangkok’s markets when finances allow, and they hope to someday explore Bangkok’s markets when finances allow.
payload.culinary_persona: Bibhuti Raut enjoys hearty meals of rice, fish curry seasoned with mustard oil and tamarind, savors Odia staples like pakhala and rasgulla, relishes Telugu biryani with a side of raita, and often indulges in street‑side puchka, though they rarely cook at home.
payload.persona: Bibhuti Raut is a diligent window cleaner whose artistic curiosity fuels a love for music, sketching and cricket, while their organized mindset and modest frugality keep them grounded, and they stay humble in every task.
payload.cultural_background: Bibhuti Raut hails from a Telugu‑speaking family that settled in the coastal district of Ganjam, Odisha. Growing up in an urban environment, he blends the cultural practices of his Telugu roots—celebrating festivals like Ugadi and Sankranti, enjoying Carnatic music and traditional dance—with the local Odia customs he encounters daily. As a member of the Raut community, he identifies with modest, working‑class values and a strong sense of communal solidarity.
payload.linguistic_background: His mother tongue is Telugu, which he can speak, read, and write at a primary‑school level. Living in Ganjam, he has picked up functional Odia for everyday communication, though he is not formally literate in it. He also understands spoken Hindi from television and local interactions, but his reading and writing skills in Hindi are limited.
payload.skills_and_expertise: His primary expertise lies in window cleaning, where he combines physical stamina with meticulous attention to detail. He is adept at using ladders and safety gear, follows high‑rise safety protocols, and performs routine maintenance of cleaning equipment. Organized and methodical, he plans his daily routes efficiently, manages time well, and ensures consistent service quality. Bibhuti's curiosity about art sharpens his observational skills, enabling him to notice even subtle dust patterns and surface imperfections. He also possesses good interpersonal skills for brief customer interactions, and basic troubleshooting abilities for minor tool repairs.
payload.skills_and_expertise_list: ["Window cleaning", "High‑rise safety protocol compliance", "Use of ladders and safety gear", "Routine equipment maintenance", "Time management", "Route planning", "Attention to detail", "Observational skills", "Customer interaction", "Basic tool troubleshooting"]
payload.hobbies_and_interests: Bibhuti enjoys listening to Telugu film songs and Odia folk music, which fuel his love for rhythm and melody. He often watches regional movies and occasionally sketches simple drawings inspired by the cityscape and temple architecture he encounters during his work. Playing cricket with friends in local grounds, taking evening walks to observe street art, and reading short stories in Telugu are his favorite ways to unwind. He also has a keen interest in learning basic painting techniques through community art workshops whenever time permits.
payload.hobbies_and_interests_list: ["Listening to Telugu and Odia music", "Watching regional movies", "Sketching cityscapes and temple architecture", "Playing cricket with friends", "Evening walks observing street art", "Reading Telugu short stories", "Participating in community art workshops"]
payload.career_goals_and_ambitions: Bibhuti aims to move beyond his current role as a window cleaner by acquiring formal certification in occupational safety and building maintenance. Within the next few years, he aspires to become a supervisor of a cleaning crew, allowing him to oversee larger projects and mentor junior workers. In the longer term, he dreams of starting his own small cleaning service business, which would provide a steadier income for his family and enable him to fund further studies in a vocational trade such as carpentry or plumbing. He also hopes to deepen his appreciation for art by attending community workshops whenever possible.

payload.system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Use ONLY allow-list TYPE names. Billing aliases: EMAIL/CONTACT_EMAIL → EMAIL_ADDRESS; ADDRESS_NUMBER → RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS; RECEIPT_NUMBER and STATE are NOT types (receipt no. / state stay plain prose; geography uses DISTRICT).

- PAN must match ^[A-Z]{5}[0-9]{4}[A-Z]$ e.g. ABCDE1234F — not KAAPR1234C-style junk.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Maternal_health must be age-plausible: routine ANC/delivery for ages ~18–40; ages 40–42 → high-risk ANC / PNC / contraception — never invent extreme late pregnancy (e.g. 49yo as routine 28-week gestation).

- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER
